techniques.name: Signal Fire
techniques.subtitle: Three fires in a triangle = universal distress

steps.instruction: Build 3 fires in a triangle, 100 feet apart.
steps.helpDetail: Three of anything in a triangle is the international distress signal. Place on high ground, clearings, or near open water for maximum visibility.

steps.instruction: Prepare each fire with dry tinder and kindling, ready to light.
steps.helpDetail: Have them pre-built so you can light quickly when you hear/see aircraft. Keep fire-starting materials dry and ready.

steps.instruction: Add green branches or wet material for heavy smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Smoke is visible from much greater distance than flame. Green leaves, wet grass, or rubber produce thick white/black smoke.

steps.instruction: At night, flames are more visible than smoke.
steps.helpDetail: At night, keep fires burning with dry wood for bright flames. During day, switch to smoky materials.

techniques.name: Signal Mirror
techniques.subtitle: Visible 50+ miles on a clear day

steps.instruction: Any reflective surface works: mirror, CD, phone screen, metal.
steps.helpDetail: Commercial signal mirrors are the best but ANY reflective object can work. Even a belt buckle or aluminum foil.

steps.instruction: Hold the mirror near your eye and extend your other hand toward the target.
steps.helpDetail: The target is the aircraft, boat, or distant point you want to signal.

steps.instruction: Reflect sunlight onto your extended hand, then move hand to align flash with target.
steps.helpDetail: The bright spot on your hand tells you where the reflection is pointing. Move it until it's on the target. Sweep back and forth across the target.

steps.instruction: Flash in sets of three (SOS pattern) — three short, three long, three short.
steps.helpDetail: Keep signaling even if you think the aircraft hasn't seen you. Pilots scan with binoculars and may see you long before you realize it.

techniques.name: Ground-to-Air Signals
techniques.subtitle: Large visible markers for aircraft

steps.instruction: Create symbols at least 10 feet tall, 3 feet wide.
steps.helpDetail: Bigger is always better. Use contrasting materials against the ground — dark rocks on light sand, light clothing on dark soil.

steps.instruction: Standard signals: V = need assistance, X = need medical help, → = traveling this direction.
steps.helpDetail: I = need medical supplies. F = need food and water. LL = all is well. These are ICAO standard ground-to-air visual signals.

steps.instruction: Place in open areas visible from above — clearings, hilltops, beaches.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid placing under tree canopy. Signals must be visible from directly above and at angles.

steps.instruction: Add shadow-casting height for visibility from low angles.
steps.helpDetail: Stack rocks, logs, or mound dirt to create 3D signals that cast shadows. Shadows dramatically increase visibility.

techniques.name: Whistle Signal
techniques.subtitle: Three blasts = help needed

steps.instruction: Three sharp blasts = universal distress signal.
steps.helpDetail: Three of anything (blasts, fires, signals) means 'help needed.' Pause 30 seconds between sets. Repeat every 5 minutes.

steps.instruction: Blow in short, sharp bursts — not long continuous blows.
steps.helpDetail: Short blasts carry farther and are easier to distinguish from natural sounds. Each blast should last about 1 second.

steps.instruction: Point the whistle toward open areas, valleys, or potential rescuer direction.
steps.helpDetail: Sound travels farther down valleys and across water. Face the most likely direction of help. Avoid blowing into dense forest — trees absorb sound.

steps.instruction: Conserve energy: blow every 5 minutes, not continuously.
steps.helpDetail: Continuous blowing wastes energy and can cause hyperventilation. Set a schedule: 3 blasts, wait 5 minutes, repeat.

steps.instruction: If no whistle: use two rocks struck together or a metal object.
steps.helpDetail: Banging rocks creates a sharp, carrying sound unnatural to the wilderness. A metal pot or cup struck with a rock is even louder. Three strikes = distress.

techniques.name: SOS in Morse Code
techniques.subtitle: · · · — — — · · · (3 short, 3 long, 3 short)

steps.instruction: SOS = 3 short, 3 long, 3 short. Any medium: light, sound, flag.
steps.helpDetail: Short = quick flash/tap. Long = 3× the duration of short. This is the most universally recognized distress signal.

steps.instruction: With a flashlight: quick-quick-quick, looong-looong-looong, quick-quick-quick.
steps.helpDetail: Aim at aircraft, boats, or distant camps. Repeat continuously. Even a phone screen works.

steps.instruction: Write SOS in sand, snow, or any visible surface.
steps.helpDetail: Make letters as large as possible. Use contrasting materials. SOS is understood in every language worldwide.

techniques.name: Maximizing Phone Battery in Emergency
techniques.subtitle: Get the most from your dying phone

steps.instruction: Turn on airplane mode immediately.
steps.helpDetail: The #1 battery drain is your phone searching for cell signal. In wilderness with no signal, it searches continuously, killing the battery.

steps.instruction: Turn off airplane mode ONLY to attempt calls/texts, then back on.
steps.helpDetail: Climb to high ground, turn off airplane mode, attempt contact, then turn it back on. Each attempt should be deliberate.

steps.instruction: Send texts instead of calling — texts use far less power.
steps.helpDetail: Texts also retry automatically and can get through with weaker signals than voice calls.

steps.instruction: Emergency numbers (112, 911, 999, etc.) work on any network and often without a SIM.
steps.helpDetail: Emergency calls route through ANY available carrier, not just yours. 112 works in most countries worldwide. Check your local number in this app's Emergency Directory.

steps.instruction: Before battery dies: text your GPS coordinates to someone.
steps.helpDetail: Open maps app, copy coordinates, text to contact. Even if the text doesn't send immediately, it will queue and send when signal is found.

techniques.name: Reading & Sharing GPS Coordinates
techniques.subtitle: Your exact location in numbers

steps.instruction: On iPhone: Open Compass app — coordinates shown at bottom.
steps.helpDetail: GPS works WITHOUT cell service or WiFi. It uses satellites directly. Your phone can always determine your position.

steps.instruction: On Android: Open Google Maps, tap the blue dot, coordinates appear.
steps.helpDetail: Or download an offline GPS app before your trip. Offline maps + GPS = full navigation without service.

steps.instruction: Share coordinates as decimal degrees: e.g., 37.7749, -122.4194.
steps.helpDetail: Decimal degrees are the most universal and least error-prone format. Include the negative sign for south/west coordinates.

steps.instruction: What3Words: every 3m×3m square has a unique 3-word address.
steps.helpDetail: The app works offline. Example: ///filled.count.soap. Emergency services in many countries accept What3Words addresses.

techniques.name: Self-Rescue Decision Making
techniques.subtitle: Stay or go? The critical decision.

steps.instruction: Stop. Breathe. Think before acting.
steps.helpDetail: Panic is the leading cause of drowning in capable swimmers. Take 10 seconds to assess: how far is shore? What direction is the current? Do you have anything that floats?

steps.instruction: Float on your back to rest and conserve energy.
steps.helpDetail: Roll onto your back, spread arms wide, arch your back slightly. Even non-swimmers can float this way. Breathe slowly and deeply — full lungs increase buoyancy.

steps.instruction: Remove heavy items: shoes, backpack, heavy jacket.
steps.helpDetail: Heavy clothing and gear drag you down. Remove boots and heavy outer layers. Keep lightweight clothing on — it provides some insulation and doesn't weigh much when swimming.

steps.instruction: Swim at 45-degree angle to current toward nearest shore.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT fight current directly. Swim diagonally. Use sidestroke or breaststroke (most energy efficient). Rest on your back between efforts.

steps.instruction: If exhausted, switch to survival floating: face down, lift head to breathe.
steps.helpDetail: Hang vertically with face in water, arms and legs dangling. Lift head to breathe every 10-15 seconds. This uses almost zero energy and you can last for hours.

techniques.name: Leaving a Trail for Rescuers
techniques.subtitle: Breadcrumbs that save your life

steps.instruction: Build rock cairns (stacked stones) at regular intervals.
steps.helpDetail: 3 rocks stacked is unnatural enough to catch a searcher's eye. Place at decision points (trail junctions, river crossings).

steps.instruction: Break branches at eye level pointing your direction.
steps.helpDetail: Snap a branch so it dangles without falling off. The broken end points your direction of travel.

steps.instruction: Tie bright cloth strips to vegetation.
steps.helpDetail: Cut strips from clothing. Tie at eye level. Use the brightest material available. Space every 100-200 yards.

steps.instruction: Drag a stick or mark the ground in soft terrain.
steps.helpDetail: In mud, sand, or snow — drag a line or make arrows. These are visible from the air and ground.

techniques.name: Helicopter Landing Zone Prep
techniques.subtitle: Clear a safe landing area

steps.instruction: Clear a flat area at least 60×60 feet (20×20 meters).
steps.helpDetail: Remove loose debris, branches, and anything that could blow into the rotor. Mark the perimeter with rocks or logs.

steps.instruction: Remove all overhead obstructions — trees, wires, branches.
steps.helpDetail: Helicopter rotors extend beyond the fuselage. Any overhead contact is catastrophic. Clear completely.

steps.instruction: Mark wind direction with a flag, smoke, or streamers.
steps.helpDetail: Helicopters prefer to land into the wind. Hang a cloth strip or light a small smoky fire at the edge of the landing zone.

steps.instruction: Stay upwind and crouch when helicopter approaches.
steps.helpDetail: The downwash is extreme — loose items become projectiles. Shield your eyes. Never approach from the tail rotor side. Wait for pilot signal.

techniques.name: Morse Code SOS
techniques.subtitle: Universal distress: ... --- ...

steps.instruction: SOS: three short, three long, three short.
steps.helpDetail: ... --- ... (dit dit dit / dah dah dah / dit dit dit). Universal distress.

steps.instruction: Signal with flashlight, mirror, whistle, or tapping.
steps.helpDetail: Short = quick flash/tap. Long = 3x duration of short.

steps.instruction: Repeat with 1-minute pauses between sets.
steps.helpDetail: Signal 1 minute, rest 1 minute. Conserve energy.

techniques.name: Helicopter Landing Zone
techniques.subtitle: Clear 100-foot zone for helicopter rescue

steps.instruction: Clear a flat area at least 100x100 feet.
steps.helpDetail: Remove loose debris. Rotor wash launches objects at lethal speed.

steps.instruction: Mark with bright material. H = need helicopter. V = need help.
steps.helpDetail: X means cannot move. Use clothing, tarp, or signal panels.

steps.instruction: Indicate wind direction with smoke or flag.
steps.helpDetail: Helicopters land into wind. Light smoky fire upwind of LZ.

steps.instruction: Stand upwind, crouch when approaching. Never approach from rear.
steps.helpDetail: Tail rotor kills. Approach front/side only when pilot signals. Stay low.

techniques.name: Trail Blazing Markers
techniques.subtitle: Leave a trail others can follow

steps.instruction: Break branches at shoulder height pointing your direction.
steps.helpDetail: Pale broken wood visible from distance. Point break in travel direction.

steps.instruction: Stack 3 rocks (cairn) at every turn or decision point.
steps.helpDetail: Three stacked rocks is universal trail marker.

steps.instruction: Scratch arrows on trees or dirt at junctions.
steps.helpDetail: Use knife, sharp rock, or stick. Eye level on trees.

steps.instruction: Tie bright cloth strips to branches every 50-100 feet.
steps.helpDetail: Tear clothing if needed. Visible in both directions.

techniques.name: International Ground Signal Codes
techniques.subtitle: V, X, I — pilot-readable from 1000 feet

steps.instruction: V = Need Help. X = Need Medical. I = Need Supplies.
steps.helpDetail: ICAO standard signals. Make at least 10 feet tall.

steps.instruction: Arrow = traveling this direction. Y = Yes. N = No.
steps.helpDetail: Answer air queries with Y and N on ground.

steps.instruction: Build with rocks, logs, clothing, or trenches.
steps.helpDetail: Maximum contrast with ground color.

steps.instruction: Minimum 10 feet long, 3 feet wide. Bigger is better.
steps.helpDetail: 3-foot letters invisible from 1000 feet. Think highway-scale.

techniques.name: Colored Smoke Signals
techniques.subtitle: Daytime signaling with combustion

steps.instruction: Build a standard fire and get it burning hot.
steps.helpDetail: You need a strong updraft to carry the smoke high.

steps.instruction: add GREEN vegetation for WHITE smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Green leaves, moss, or grass produce thick white smoke. Good against dark backgrounds (trees).

steps.instruction: Add OIL, RUBBER, or PLASTIC for BLACK smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Tires, oil-soaked rags, or plastic produce black smoke. Good against light backgrounds (snow, sand).

steps.instruction: Create 3 distinct puffs (cover/uncover) for SOS.
steps.helpDetail: Use a blanket or coat. Cover the fire, let smoke build, release. Three puffs is the international distress signal.

techniques.name: Personal Locator Beacon (PLB)
techniques.subtitle: One button sends your GPS to rescue coordination

steps.instruction: Deploy the antenna fully and activate the beacon.
steps.helpDetail: Most PLBs have a spring-loaded antenna and a protected activation button. Remove the cover, extend antenna, press and hold for 1 second.

steps.instruction: Place beacon with clear sky view — no canopy, no overhangs.
steps.helpDetail: PLBs transmit on 406 MHz to COSPAS-SARSAT satellites. Signal needs unobstructed path to sky. Even partial canopy degrades signal.

steps.instruction: Leave activated for minimum 24 hours. Do not turn off and on.
steps.helpDetail: Multiple satellite passes improve position accuracy from 5km to 100m. Intermittent activation confuses the system.

steps.instruction: Stay near the beacon. Rescuers will come to the signal location.
steps.helpDetail: If you move, carry the beacon. Response times: 1-4 hours in populated areas, 8-24 hours in remote wilderness.

techniques.name: VHF Radio Distress Call
techniques.subtitle: Channel 16: international distress frequency

steps.instruction: Tune to Channel 16 (156.800 MHz) — monitored by Coast Guard worldwide.
steps.helpDetail: Channel 16 is the international calling and distress frequency. All marine vessels and coast guard stations monitor it 24/7.

steps.instruction: Press transmit and say: MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, this is [your name/vessel].
steps.helpDetail: MAYDAY = life-threatening emergency. PAN-PAN = urgent but not life-threatening. SECURITE = safety information. Use MAYDAY for survival situations.

steps.instruction: State: position, nature of emergency, number of people, assistance needed.
steps.helpDetail: Example: 'Position 37.7749 north, 122.4194 west. Party of 3, one injured. Need helicopter evacuation. Over.'

steps.instruction: Release transmit button and listen for response. Repeat every 2 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: VHF range is line-of-sight: 5-25 miles depending on terrain and antenna height. Move to high ground to extend range.

techniques.name: Night Signaling Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Be visible when darkness hides everything

steps.instruction: Maintain a bright fire on the highest accessible point.
steps.helpDetail: A hilltop fire is visible for 20+ miles on a clear night. Use dry hardwood for sustained bright flames, not smoldering coals.

steps.instruction: Use flashlight or phone flash in SOS pattern.
steps.helpDetail: Three short flashes, three long flashes, three short flashes. Repeat. Even a dim light is visible for miles at night.

steps.instruction: Create a torch from resinous wood or cloth soaked in fat.
steps.helpDetail: Birch bark, pine fatwood, or any cloth with animal fat/oil burns brightly. Wave the torch in a figure-8 pattern for attention.

steps.instruction: Use reflective material to catch any light source.
steps.helpDetail: Emergency blankets, foil, even wet metal surfaces reflect moonlight and starlight. Hang them at angles facing likely rescue directions.

techniques.name: Vehicle-Based Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Turn your vehicle into a rescue beacon

steps.instruction: Stay with the vehicle — it is more visible than you are.
steps.helpDetail: A car is visible from aircraft. A person in the wilderness is nearly invisible. Your vehicle is your best signal, shelter, and resource.

steps.instruction: Open hood and trunk — this signals distress to aircraft.
steps.helpDetail: An open hood is a recognized distress signal. Tie bright fabric to the antenna or mirror. Arrange floor mats on the roof as signals.

steps.instruction: Use mirrors, headlights, horn for active signaling.
steps.helpDetail: Side mirrors angle sunlight. Headlights flash at night. Horn blasts carry far. Conserve battery — use in bursts when rescue is likely.

steps.instruction: Burn oil or tires for thick black smoke (extreme emergency only).
steps.helpDetail: Drain oil onto dirt, ignite with battery spark. Black smoke is visible for miles against any sky. Use only when rescue is imminent.

techniques.name: Improvised Flag Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Attract attention with movement and color

steps.instruction: Tie the brightest fabric you have to a long pole or branch.
steps.helpDetail: Orange, red, or yellow are most visible against natural backgrounds. Even a white undershirt works against dark terrain.

steps.instruction: Wave in large, slow arcs — both arms overhead means 'need help.'
steps.helpDetail: Both arms raised and waving is the international ground-to-air distress gesture. One arm raised means 'all okay.'

steps.instruction: Plant the flag on the highest visible point when not actively waving.
steps.helpDetail: Wind keeps it moving, which catches attention. Moving objects are detected 10x faster than static ones by human vision.

techniques.name: Drone Search Awareness
techniques.subtitle: Modern SAR uses drones — know how to be found

steps.instruction: Maximize thermal contrast — drones often use infrared cameras.
steps.helpDetail: Your body heat stands out against cold ground. Clear surroundings of insulating debris. Lie in an open area if you hear a drone.

steps.instruction: Create large, clear ground signals visible from directly above.
steps.helpDetail: Drones fly lower than aircraft but look straight down. Make signals at least 6 feet in any dimension with maximum color contrast.

steps.instruction: Wave reflective materials — drones detect moving objects and reflections.
steps.helpDetail: Emergency blankets, phone screens, anything shiny. Movement triggers drone operator attention faster than static signals.

techniques.name: Satellite Messenger Usage
techniques.subtitle: Two-way emergency communication anywhere on Earth

steps.instruction: Activate SOS function — press and hold the dedicated SOS button.
steps.helpDetail: Garmin inReach, SPOT, Zoleo all have dedicated SOS buttons. This connects to GEOS International Emergency Response Center.

steps.instruction: Send a preset message with your GPS coordinates.
steps.helpDetail: Pre-configure emergency contacts and messages before your trip. Include medical conditions, group size, and planned route.

steps.instruction: Use two-way messaging to communicate situation details.
steps.helpDetail: Unlike PLBs, satellite messengers allow back-and-forth communication. You can describe injuries, conditions, and accessibility for rescue teams.

steps.instruction: Conserve battery — turn off tracking, reduce message frequency.
steps.helpDetail: Most satellite messengers last 24-100 hours. Turn off interval tracking. Send messages only when needed. Keep warm to preserve battery.

techniques.name: Throw Bag River Rescue
techniques.subtitle: Reach a person in moving water

steps.instruction: Yell to victim: 'GRAB THE BAG!' Get their attention first.
steps.helpDetail: A person in panic may not see or hear you. Make eye contact. Use sharp whistle blasts. Wave arms. They need to be ready to grab.

steps.instruction: Open the bag, hold the end of the rope.
steps.helpDetail: Keep one foot on the rope tail or tie it to your wrist. If you throw the bag without holding the rope end, you've lost your rescue tool.

steps.instruction: Throw underhanded PAST the victim, so rope drapes across them.
steps.helpDetail: Aim beyond and slightly upstream of the victim. Throwing past them means the rope will drape across their body — easier to grab than catching a bag.

steps.instruction: Once they grab the rope, brace yourself and pull.
steps.helpDetail: Sit down, dig heels in, lean back. The current will push them toward you as you maintain tension. Do NOT tie rope to yourself — you could be pulled in.

steps.instruction: If first throw misses: re-stuff bag quickly or throw loose coils.
steps.helpDetail: Quickly stuff rope back into bag (doesn't need to be neat). Or gather rope in loose coils and throw the coils. Speed matters more than technique on the second throw.

techniques.name: Cliff and Canyon Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Signal from terrain that blocks normal methods

steps.instruction: Use sound amplification — canyon walls reflect and amplify whistles.
steps.helpDetail: A whistle in a canyon can be heard miles further than in open terrain. Sound bounces between walls creating natural amplification.

steps.instruction: Create signals on the canyon rim if possible, not the floor.
steps.helpDetail: Canyon floors are invisible from the air and from distance. Any signal on the rim is worth 10 signals on the floor.

steps.instruction: Use smoke — it rises out of canyons and is visible from above.
steps.helpDetail: Smoke is the one signal type that works from canyon bottoms. Hot air rises, carrying smoke up and out of the canyon.

techniques.name: Improvised Flotation Device
techniques.subtitle: Stay afloat with what you have

steps.instruction: Pants flotation: tie off leg openings, inflate by scooping air.
steps.helpDetail: Remove pants, tie each leg end in a knot, hold waist open and scoop over your head to trap air. Provides 15-20 minutes of buoyancy.

steps.instruction: Use any sealed container — water bottles, dry bags, sealed coolers.
steps.helpDetail: Two empty 2-liter bottles can support an adult's head above water. Stuff clothing into a garbage bag for a makeshift float.

steps.instruction: Logs, branches, and debris make effective rafts with cordage.
steps.helpDetail: Even a single thick log supports significant weight. Green wood floats well. Lash with rope, belts, vines, or torn fabric strips.

steps.instruction: Survival float: face down, lift head only to breathe.
steps.helpDetail: Also called 'dead man's float.' Arms and legs hang relaxed. Lift face to inhale, then relax. Uses almost zero energy. Can sustain for hours.

techniques.name: Ground-to-Air Signal Panel
techniques.subtitle: Create large visual signals visible from aircraft

steps.instruction: Create a large V (need help), X (need medical), or arrow (traveling this direction).
steps.helpDetail: International ground-to-air signals: V = need assistance, X = need medical help, arrow = traveling this direction, I = need supplies, F = need food/water, Y = yes, N = no.

steps.instruction: Make signals at least 10 feet long using contrasting materials.
steps.helpDetail: Use branches, rocks, clothing, or dig trenches in soil. CONTRAST is key: dark materials on light ground, light materials on dark ground. Shadows from trenches or piled rocks are visible from high altitude.

steps.instruction: Place in open areas — meadows, beaches, ridgetops. Away from tree canopy.
steps.helpDetail: Aircraft cannot see through tree canopy. Place signals in the largest open area available. Multiple signals increase detection probability. Maintain signals daily — wind and weather degrade them.

techniques.name: Survival Whistle Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Use standardized whistle patterns for rescue communication

steps.instruction: Three blasts = universal distress signal. Pause. Repeat.
steps.helpDetail: Three of anything is the international distress signal: 3 whistle blasts, 3 fires, 3 gunshots. Blow three sharp blasts, pause 1 minute, repeat. A whistle carries 1-2 miles — much farther than shouting.

steps.instruction: Two blasts = 'come to me' or acknowledgment from rescuers.
steps.helpDetail: If you hear 2 blasts in response to your 3, rescuers have heard you and are coming. Continue blowing 3 blasts periodically so they can home in on your position.

steps.instruction: One long blast = 'attention' or 'where are you?'
steps.helpDetail: Used to get attention or locate group members. A whistle is the single most important signaling device to carry. It works when you're too exhausted to shout. It works in fog, darkness, and wind.

techniques.name: Water Rescue Throw Line
techniques.subtitle: Rescue someone from water without entering it yourself

steps.instruction: Throw a rope, cord, or improvised line to the person in the water.
steps.helpDetail: Rule: REACH, THROW, ROW, GO (in that order of safety). Try to reach with a stick first. Then throw a line. Only enter water as absolute last resort — drowning victims pull rescuers under.

steps.instruction: Attach a flotation device to the line if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Tie a water bottle, sealed container, or life jacket to the end. This gives them something to grab and provides buoyancy. Throw PAST the victim and pull line across them.

steps.instruction: Brace yourself firmly and pull them in slowly.
steps.helpDetail: Sit down, brace feet against rocks or trees. A panicking victim pulls with surprising force. Pull steadily — don't let them pull you in. Talk calmly and firmly: 'Hold on. I'm pulling you in.'

techniques.name: Vehicle Emergency Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Use a disabled vehicle as a rescue beacon

steps.instruction: Stay with the vehicle — it's much easier to spot than a person.
steps.helpDetail: A car is visible from aircraft at 10+ miles. A person on foot is visible from less than 1 mile. Your vehicle provides shelter, signaling tools, and materials. The #1 cause of vehicle-stranding deaths is leaving the vehicle.

steps.instruction: Use mirrors, hood, CDs, or any reflective surface to signal aircraft.
steps.helpDetail: Side mirrors can be removed for hand-signaling. Open the hood — it's an unusual sight that attracts attention. Hang bright clothing on the antenna. Lay out floor mats in an X pattern nearby.

steps.instruction: If safe, burn the spare tire for black smoke signal (extreme last resort).
steps.helpDetail: Tire fires produce thick black smoke visible for 20+ miles. Only burn if rescue is actively being sought and you need rapid attention. Keep fire away from the fuel tank. Flash headlights at night.

techniques.name: Cold Water Self-Rescue
techniques.subtitle: Survive cold water immersion using the 1-10-1 principle

steps.instruction: Control your breathing: fight the cold shock gasp reflex.
steps.helpDetail: Cold water triggers involuntary gasping (cold shock response). This lasts 1-3 minutes. Focus on slow, deliberate breaths. If you gasp underwater, you will inhale water and drown.

steps.instruction: Float on your back. Do NOT swim unless shore is within 50 meters.
steps.helpDetail: Swimming accelerates heat loss 25x faster than floating still. Conserve energy. If wearing a PFD, assume the HELP position (Heat Escape Lessening Posture): knees to chest, arms crossed.

steps.instruction: Keep your head above water at all costs.
steps.helpDetail: You lose 40-50% of body heat through your head. Tilt head back, keep face to the sky. If waves are splashing your face, turn your back to the waves.

steps.instruction: Grab onto any floating debris or solid object.
steps.helpDetail: Wood, coolers, overturned boats, dock pilings — anything that reduces your effort to stay afloat. Drape arms over it, don't grip (grip strength fails quickly in cold).

steps.instruction: Minimize movement. Wait for rescue or drift to shore.
steps.helpDetail: You have 10-15 minutes of meaningful swimming ability in cold water before muscles fail. After that, focused stillness and flotation are your only survival tools. Blow a whistle or yell periodically to attract attention.

techniques.name: Helicopter Rescue Procedures
techniques.subtitle: Safely interact with rescue helicopters

steps.instruction: Clear a landing zone: 100x100 feet minimum, flat, no loose debris.
steps.helpDetail: Remove loose branches, clothing, tarps — rotor wash sends everything flying at high speed. Mark wind direction with smoke or a flag. Helicopter lands INTO the wind. Mark the zone with bright items at corners.

steps.instruction: Signal the helicopter: both arms raised in a Y = need help. One arm up = all OK.
steps.helpDetail: Y (both arms raised) = Yes, I need help. One arm raised, one down = all OK / negative. Waving one arm side to side = distress. Do NOT wave both arms wildly — it can mean 'don't land.'

steps.instruction: Approach from the FRONT and DOWNHILL side. NEVER approach from behind (tail rotor).
steps.helpDetail: The tail rotor is nearly invisible and instantly lethal. Always approach where the pilot can see you. Stay low — rotor blades dip. On slopes, approach from the downhill side. Wait for crew signal before approaching.

techniques.name: Smoke Signal Timing & Technique
techniques.subtitle: Maximize smoke signal effectiveness for aerial detection

steps.instruction: Build three fires in a triangle — three smoke columns = distress signal.
steps.helpDetail: Space fires 100-200 feet apart in an equilateral triangle. Three columns of smoke is the international distress signal. One column of smoke looks like a campfire. Three is unmistakably a call for help.

steps.instruction: Use green vegetation for white smoke on dark backgrounds, tires/oil for dark smoke on light backgrounds.
steps.helpDetail: Match smoke color to background for contrast. Against dark forest: white smoke (green branches, moss, wet leaves). Against light sky/snow: dark smoke (rubber, oil, plastic). Contrast = visibility.

steps.instruction: Keep signal fires prepared but unlit. Light when you see or hear aircraft.
steps.helpDetail: Pre-build signal fires with tinder and kindling ready. Put a cover over them to keep dry. When you detect aircraft, light all three quickly. Keep green branches nearby to throw on for smoke.

techniques.name: Signal Kite Construction
techniques.subtitle: Build a kite to increase visual presence above tree canopy

steps.instruction: Build a diamond kite frame from two crossed sticks.
steps.helpDetail: Vertical stick: 36 inches. Horizontal stick: 24 inches. Cross them at 1/3 from the top of the vertical stick. Lash the intersection tightly. Notch the ends of each stick.

steps.instruction: Cover with plastic bag, poncho, or large leaves tied to the frame.
steps.helpDetail: String cord through the notches to create the outline. Stretch plastic or fabric over the frame and secure. Use the brightest colored material available. A tail of cloth strips helps stability.

steps.instruction: Attach a long line and fly in open area. Tie bright streamers to the line.
steps.helpDetail: A kite flying 100+ feet above tree canopy is visible for miles. Attach reflective tape, bright fabric, or foil pieces to the line. Even in moderate wind, a properly built kite extends your visual signature dramatically.

techniques.name: Urban Search & Rescue Marking
techniques.subtitle: Use FEMA X-marking to communicate with rescue teams

steps.instruction: Draw a large X on the outside of the building entrance.
steps.helpDetail: This is the FEMA Disaster Marking System. The X divides into 4 quadrants. Use spray paint, chalk, or marker. Make it large enough to see from the street.

steps.instruction: Fill quadrants: top = team ID, left = date/time, right = hazards, bottom = victims found.
steps.helpDetail: Top: your identifier. Left: date and time of search. Right: hazards present (rats, gas leak, structural damage). Bottom: number of live/dead victims (e.g., '2L/1D' = 2 living, 1 dead).

steps.instruction: A single slash means search in progress. Complete X means search finished.
steps.helpDetail: When you begin searching: draw one slash (\). When complete: add the second slash to complete the X and fill in quadrants. This prevents duplicate searches and communicates status to other teams.

techniques.name: River Self-Rescue Position
techniques.subtitle: Survive being swept away in river current

steps.instruction: Roll onto your back, feet pointing downstream.
steps.helpDetail: This is the defensive swimming position. Your feet act as bumpers against rocks. Keep your hips up and back arched slightly. NEVER swim headfirst downstream.

steps.instruction: Use feet to push off rocks and obstacles.
steps.helpDetail: Keep legs slightly bent as shock absorbers. Push off rocks with your feet as you approach them. Never try to stand in fast-moving water — foot entrapment kills.

steps.instruction: Look downstream for eddies (calm water behind rocks).
steps.helpDetail: Eddies form behind large rocks and on the inside of river bends. The water circulates upstream in eddies — you can rest there. Swim aggressively toward them.

steps.instruction: To reach shore: ferry angle at 45 degrees across current.
steps.helpDetail: Roll onto your stomach (aggressive swim position) and swim at a 45-degree angle toward shore, pointing upstream. The current will push you diagonally toward the bank.

steps.instruction: Avoid strainers (fallen trees, debris piles) at all costs.
steps.helpDetail: Strainers let water pass through but trap bodies. If you cannot avoid one, swim TOWARD it aggressively and try to climb up and over it. Never turn your back to a strainer.

techniques.name: Self-Evacuation Decision Making
techniques.subtitle: Decide when to stay put vs. self-rescue

steps.instruction: Stay put IF: someone knows your location, you filed a trip plan, you're injured, or terrain is dangerous.
steps.helpDetail: Search and rescue finds stationary targets much faster than moving ones. If anyone knows where you are, staying put is almost always the better choice. You're easier to find, you conserve energy, and you reduce injury risk.

steps.instruction: Self-evacuate IF: no one knows you're missing, you know your location, and you're uninjured.
steps.helpDetail: If nobody will report you missing, nobody will search for you. In this case, self-rescue is your only option. You must know approximately where you are and which direction leads to help.

steps.instruction: Leave clear signs at your camp indicating direction of travel.
steps.helpDetail: If you leave camp: leave a note (under a rock, protected from weather), build arrows pointing your direction, mark trees along your route. This lets rescuers follow you if a search begins after you leave.

techniques.name: Improvised Litter Carry Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Transport an injured person without commercial equipment

steps.instruction: Build a pole litter: two poles + fabric/jacket threaded between them.
steps.helpDetail: Two sturdy poles 7-8 feet long. Thread jackets (zipped closed, arms inside) onto the poles. Or use a blanket/tarp folded around the poles. Test with your own weight before loading the patient.

steps.instruction: For one-person carry: use a backpack frame as a seat carry.
steps.helpDetail: Patient sits on a large backpack carried on your back. Their legs hang over your hips. Secure with straps. This works for conscious patients with lower extremity injuries.

steps.instruction: Carry team rotation: switch every 200 meters to prevent exhaustion.
steps.helpDetail: A 150-lb patient on a litter is exhausting. Rotate carriers every 200 meters or every 5 minutes. Need minimum 4 carriers (2 working, 2 resting). Over rough terrain, 6-8 carriers is ideal.

techniques.name: Ground-to-Air Body Signals
techniques.subtitle: Use body positions to communicate with aircraft

steps.instruction: Both arms raised above head, forming a Y = 'YES' / 'I need help'.
steps.helpDetail: This is the universal distress signal recognized by all search aircraft. Stand in an open clearing for maximum visibility.
steps.illustrationDescription: Both arms raised above head, f

steps.instruction: One arm raised, one down diagonally = 'NO' / 'I do not need assistance'.
steps.helpDetail: Used when aircraft investigate but you are not in distress. Important to know so you don't accidentally signal distress.
steps.illustrationDescription: One arm raised, one down diago

steps.instruction: Lie flat face-up with arms and legs spread (X shape) = 'Unable to proceed / need medical help'.
steps.helpDetail: This signals a medical emergency. The X shape is visible from high altitude and distinguishable from standing signals.
steps.illustrationDescription: Lie flat face-up with arms and

steps.instruction: Wave one arm side to side while standing = 'I see you / pick me up here'.
steps.helpDetail: Use to confirm you've seen the aircraft and to direct them to your position. Wave with a bright colored item if available.
steps.illustrationDescription: Wave one arm side to side whil

steps.instruction: Point both arms in one direction = 'Go this way' / indicating direction of other survivors.
steps.helpDetail: Used to direct rescuers toward other people who need help. Point emphatically and repeatedly in the direction.
steps.illustrationDescription: Point both arms in one directi

techniques.name: Reflective Surface Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Signal using mirrors, glass, and shiny objects

steps.instruction: Any reflective surface works: mirror, glass, CD/DVD, phone screen, aluminum foil, credit card, belt buckle.
steps.helpDetail: A signal mirror flash is visible up to 100 miles away in clear conditions. It is the single most effective daytime signaling device.

steps.instruction: Hold the reflective surface near your eye. Extend your other hand toward the target (aircraft, ship, person).
steps.helpDetail: The outstretched hand is your sighting aid. You'll guide the flash onto it, then onto the target.

steps.instruction: Angle the reflective surface to catch sunlight. Move it until the bright spot appears on your outstretched hand.
steps.helpDetail: The bright spot on your hand confirms you have the right angle. Now move the flash onto the target by lowering your hand.

steps.instruction: Flash repeatedly — 3 quick flashes followed by a pause. Repeat. This mimics SOS pattern.
steps.helpDetail: Steady light is less noticeable than flashing. The human eye is drawn to intermittent flashing. Sweep back and forth across the target.

steps.instruction: Even without a visible target, sweep the horizon periodically. Aircraft may see your flash before you see them.
steps.helpDetail: Flash toward any engine noise, even if you can't see the source. Someone 50 miles away may spot your flash before you hear their engine.

techniques.name: Tire Fire Smoke Signal
techniques.subtitle: Create thick black smoke from burning rubber

steps.instruction: Burning rubber (tires, shoe soles, rubber mats) produces thick, black smoke visible for miles.
steps.helpDetail: Black smoke stands out against sky, snow, sand, and light-colored terrain. It is one of the most visible smoke signals.

steps.instruction: Build a hot base fire first with dry wood. Once burning strongly, add rubber items.
steps.helpDetail: Rubber needs high heat to ignite and sustain combustion. A cold fire will not ignite rubber — get a strong wood fire going first.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build a hot base fire first wi

steps.instruction: If near a vehicle: use a spare tire, floor mats, or rubber hoses. Cut into pieces for controlled burning.
steps.helpDetail: Smaller pieces burn more efficiently. A whole tire produces massive smoke but is difficult to light. Cut sections work better on a small fire.
steps.illustrationDescription: If near a vehicle: use a spare

steps.instruction: Alternate rubber (black smoke) with green vegetation (white smoke) for maximum contrast.
steps.helpDetail: Two smoke signals of different colors is unmistakable. White-then-black or vice versa creates a visual pattern that screams 'intentional signal.'

steps.instruction: CAUTION: burning rubber is toxic. Stand upwind. Do NOT inhale the fumes.
steps.helpDetail: Rubber smoke contains harmful chemicals. Use only as an emergency signal method. Keep yourself and others upwind at all times.

techniques.name: Clothing Ground Signals
techniques.subtitle: Arrange clothing on the ground as visual markers

steps.instruction: Spread brightly colored clothing flat on the ground in an open clearing.
steps.helpDetail: Bright orange, red, yellow, and white contrast best against natural terrain. A single bright jacket on green grass is visible from hundreds of feet up.

steps.instruction: Arrange clothing in geometric shapes that don't occur in nature: X, V, or SOS.
steps.helpDetail: Natural debris forms random patterns. Geometric shapes are clearly artificial and attract investigation. An X is the most recognized distress marker.

steps.instruction: Make signals as large as possible — minimum 10 feet across for aerial visibility.
steps.helpDetail: Aircraft at 1000 feet altitude can see a 10-foot signal. Tie clothing together or weight with rocks to expand coverage.
steps.illustrationDescription: Make signals as large as possi

steps.instruction: Weight all items with rocks to prevent wind from blowing them away.
steps.helpDetail: Clothing is lightweight and can shift or flip. Rocks at corners and centers keep signals anchored and visible in their intended shape.
steps.illustrationDescription: Weight all items with rocks to

steps.instruction: Keep signals in place 24/7. An aircraft may overfly once — your signal must be there when it happens.
steps.helpDetail: Don't remove signals at night or in rain thinking 'no one will see.' You never know when a search aircraft will pass. Leave them permanently.

techniques.name: Sound Signal Patterns
techniques.subtitle: Use horn, whistle, or voice in recognized distress patterns

steps.instruction: International distress signal: 3 blasts of any sound, followed by a 1-minute pause, then repeat.
steps.helpDetail: Three of ANYTHING is the universal distress code — 3 fires, 3 gun shots, 3 whistle blasts, 3 flashes. Repeat every minute.

steps.instruction: Whistle: produces a sharp, far-carrying sound. Blow 3 long blasts (3 seconds each).
steps.helpDetail: A whistle carries farther than the human voice and requires less energy. A pea whistle reaches 1+ mile. A plastic whistle works wet or dry.

steps.instruction: Car horn: sound SOS in Morse code — 3 short, 3 long, 3 short. Repeat every 2 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Car horns carry 1-2 miles in quiet conditions. SOS pattern is unmistakable. Run the horn from the battery — engine doesn't need to be running.
steps.illustrationDescription: Car horn: sound SOS in Morse c

steps.instruction: Voice: yell 'HELP' in groups of three. Project from the diaphragm, don't scream.
steps.helpDetail: Screaming tires the voice quickly. Deep, projected shouts carry farther and last longer. Cup hands around mouth to direct sound.

steps.instruction: Bang rocks or sticks together for a sharp, carrying sound that travels through forest.
steps.helpDetail: Percussion sounds travel well through trees when voices are absorbed. Two flat rocks smacked together produce a sharp crack audible at distance.

techniques.name: Night Light Signals
techniques.subtitle: Signal rescuers using light sources at night

steps.instruction: Flash SOS with any light source: 3 short, 3 long, 3 short. Repeat every minute.
steps.helpDetail: SOS is the most universally recognized distress signal. Use a flashlight, phone screen, campfire (block/unblock with a blanket), or lighter.
steps.illustrationDescription: Flash SOS with any light sourc

steps.instruction: Build a signal fire on high ground. Bright flame is visible 5-15 miles at night.
steps.helpDetail: Fire is the most powerful nighttime signal. Even a small fire on a hilltop attracts attention from great distances. Build BIG.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build a signal fire on high gr

steps.instruction: Phone screen: set to maximum brightness, turn ON and OFF in SOS pattern. Even a cracked phone works.
steps.helpDetail: The white screen light is visible at surprisingly long distances at night. Even in airplane mode with a dying battery, a few minutes of signaling may work.
steps.illustrationDescription: Phone screen: set to maximum b

steps.instruction: Car headlights: flash high beams in SOS pattern. Visible for miles on flat terrain.
steps.helpDetail: Point headlights toward likely rescue directions — roads, towns, flight paths. High beams reach 1+ mile visibility.

steps.instruction: Light discipline when NOT signaling: keep your fire low and shielded if you want to avoid attention from threats.
steps.helpDetail: In a hostile situation, controlled light is essential. Signal only when you hear or see rescuers. Otherwise, keep your fire small and shielded.

techniques.name: Water Dye Marker
techniques.subtitle: Create visible markers on water surface for air rescue

steps.instruction: Commercial sea dye markers create bright green/yellow patches visible from aircraft. Use if available.
steps.helpDetail: Sea dye markers are included in most life raft survival kits. They spread to a 50+ foot diameter patch visible from high altitude.

steps.instruction: Improvised: motor oil creates a visible sheen on water. Pour around the raft or swim area.
steps.helpDetail: Oil sheen reflects sunlight differently than water, creating a visible contrast from aircraft. Even a small amount creates a noticeable patch.

steps.instruction: Fluorescein (if available in first aid kits) creates an extremely bright green marker visible from miles.
steps.helpDetail: Fluorescein is the standard water-tracing dye. A small vial creates a massive, bright green patch. Some survival kits include it.

steps.instruction: At night on water: chemical light sticks in the water create a glowing marker visible to surface and air rescuers.
steps.helpDetail: Tie glow sticks to the raft or trail them in the water. Green and yellow are most visible. Crack all available sticks for maximum glow.
steps.illustrationDescription: At night on water: chemical li

steps.instruction: Spread any brightly colored fabric flat on the water surface around the raft.
steps.helpDetail: Fabric stays visible for hours. Emergency blankets (silver/gold) create reflective markers on the water surface visible to search aircraft.

techniques.name: Improvised Litter (Stretcher)
techniques.subtitle: Build a carrying stretcher from available materials

steps.instruction: Find 2 strong poles 6-7 feet long, 2-3 inches diameter. Must support body weight without bending.
steps.helpDetail: Green hardwood is ideal — it flexes without breaking. Test each pole by pressing hard with both hands. If it cracks, find a stronger one.

steps.instruction: Method 1 (jacket): zip/button 2-3 jackets closed, thread poles through the sleeves.
steps.helpDetail: The jacket torsos become the bed. 3 jackets provide a full-length litter. Extra jackets add comfort and weight support.

steps.instruction: Method 2 (blanket): lay a blanket flat, place one pole 1/3 from the edge, fold the short side over, place second pole, fold remaining fabric over both.
steps.helpDetail: The blanket wraps around both poles, and the patient's weight on top prevents unwrapping. No tying needed.

steps.instruction: Method 3 (poles + crossbars): lash cross-sticks every 6 inches between the two poles. Weave small branches for a platform.
steps.helpDetail: This is the strongest method but takes the longest. Requires rope or vine. Results in a rigid platform suitable for spinal injury transport.

steps.instruction: Pad the litter surface. Secure the patient with strips of cloth. 4 carriers is ideal — 2 minimum.
steps.helpDetail: Strap the patient at chest, hips, and legs. Elevate the injured body part. Carriers should move in step — 'left together, right together.'

techniques.name: Ground Signal Panels
techniques.subtitle: Create standard ground-to-air signal markers

steps.instruction: V = 'Require Assistance'. Lay rocks, logs, or fabric in a V shape, 10+ feet per arm.
steps.helpDetail: The V symbol is the ICAO standard for 'need help.' It should be clearly artificial — straight lines, high contrast against terrain.

steps.instruction: X = 'Require Medical Help'. Build an X, 10+ feet per arm, using contrasting materials.
steps.helpDetail: X signals a medical emergency requiring evacuation. Build with bright materials or dark materials on light ground.

steps.instruction: I = 'Need Supplies'. A single straight line, 10+ feet long.
steps.helpDetail: This tells rescue aircraft you're alive and in position but need food, water, or medical supplies dropped.

steps.instruction: → (Arrow) = 'Traveling this direction'. Point the arrow the way you're heading.
steps.helpDetail: If you must move, leave an arrow pointing your travel direction. Rescuers will follow your indicated path.

steps.instruction: Build signals from the most contrasting materials available: dark rocks on sand, white cloth on green grass.
steps.helpDetail: Contrast is everything. A signal that blends in is useless. The bigger and higher-contrast, the more effective. Shadow-casting 3D signals work best.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build signals from the most co

techniques.name: SOS Signal Fire Triangle
techniques.subtitle: Build the international three-fire distress signal

steps.instruction: Build 3 separate fire pits in an equilateral triangle pattern, 100 feet apart.
steps.helpDetail: Three fires in a triangle is the internationally recognized distress signal. Space evenly for clear triangle shape from above.

steps.instruction: Prepare each fire pit with complete fire lay: tinder, kindling, fuel wood, and a smoke-producing layer.
steps.helpDetail: All three fires must be ready to light QUICKLY when a rescue opportunity appears. Pre-build each with ignitable tinder on top.
steps.illustrationDescription: Prepare each fire pit with com

steps.instruction: For daytime: add green branches, wet leaves, or rubber to produce thick smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Smoke signals are visible during the day when flames are not. Green conifer boughs produce white smoke. Rubber produces black smoke.

steps.instruction: For nighttime: keep fires burning brightly with dry hardwood for maximum flame visibility.
steps.helpDetail: Three bright fires in a triangle at night are visible from aircraft for miles. Feed all three fires simultaneously.

steps.instruction: Maintain signal fire readiness 24/7. You may get only one chance when an aircraft passes.
steps.helpDetail: Keep matches/lighter in a waterproof container at each fire site. Store dry tinder under cover. Practice lighting quickly.

techniques.name: Emergency Cell Phone Strategy
techniques.subtitle: Maximize a dying phone's rescue potential

steps.instruction: Enable airplane mode IMMEDIATELY to preserve battery. Only disable to make a call or send a text.
steps.helpDetail: A phone searching for signal drains battery 10x faster than airplane mode. A phone with 5% battery in airplane mode can last hours.

steps.instruction: Text instead of calling — texts use less power and can send on weaker signals.
steps.helpDetail: A text message requires only a brief connection. It can transmit on a signal too weak for voice. Text your GPS coordinates if possible.

steps.instruction: Climb to the highest elevated point before attempting a call. Even 50 feet helps signal reach.
steps.helpDetail: Cell signals travel line-of-sight. Hilltops, ridges, and treeline elevations dramatically increase your chance of connecting to a tower.

steps.instruction: Dial emergency number (911/112) — emergency calls can connect through ANY carrier's tower, including locked phones.
steps.helpDetail: You do not need active service. Emergency calls route through the nearest available tower regardless of your carrier or account status.

steps.instruction: Send location via text before phone dies: 'LOST [GPS coordinates or landmark description] [your name] [condition]'.
steps.helpDetail: Even if the call fails, the text may queue and send later. Include maximum information in minimum characters. GPS coordinates are most valuable.

techniques.name: Helicopter LZ Preparation
techniques.subtitle: Prepare a safe landing zone for medical evacuation

steps.instruction: Select a flat, level area at least 100x100 feet (30x30 meters) clear of trees and power lines.
steps.helpDetail: Helicopters need massive vertical clearance. Ensure the approach and departure paths are clear. Slope must be less than 5 degrees.
steps.illustrationDescription: Select a flat, level area at l

steps.instruction: Remove all loose debris from the LZ: tents, tarps, loose branches, hats, trash.
steps.helpDetail: Rotor wash creates hurricane-force winds (70-100mph). Loose items become lethal projectiles or can get sucked into the engine.
steps.illustrationDescription: Remove all loose debris from t

steps.instruction: Mark the four corners of the LZ and the wind direction.
steps.helpDetail: During the day: use high-visibility panels properly staked down. At night: use glow sticks or flashlights pointing inward, NEVER pointing up at the pilot.
steps.illustrationDescription: Mark the four corners of the L

steps.instruction: Indicate wind direction using a windsock, smoke (outside the LZ), or standing with your back to the wind.
steps.helpDetail: Helicopters must land facing into the wind. Pilots need to see surface wind direction before committing to the approach.

steps.instruction: When the helicopter approaches: turn away, protect your eyes, and kneel down. DO NOT approach until signaled.
steps.helpDetail: Rotor wash will blast dirt and debris. Do not stand up or approach the aircraft until the pilot or crew chief explicitly motions you forward.

techniques.name: PLB / inReach Activation Protocol
techniques.subtitle: How to maximize the effectiveness of a rescue beacon

steps.instruction: Ensure the device has a clear, 360-degree view of the sky. Move out of deep canyons or thick canopy if possible.
steps.helpDetail: PLBs communicate with satellites. Trees, rock walls, and buildings block the signal. Higher ground is always better.
steps.illustrationDescription: Ensure the device has a clear,

steps.instruction: Point the antenna straight up toward the sky. Do not lay the device flat or hold it horizontally.
steps.helpDetail: The antenna is designed to broadcast vertically. Incorrect orientation reduces the signal power reaching the satellites.

steps.instruction: Once activated, DO NOT turn it off. Keep it on and in place until rescuers arrive.
steps.helpDetail: Turning it off breaks the track and tells the rescue center the emergency is over. Leaving it on provides continuous location updates.
steps.illustrationDescription: Once activated, DO NOT turn it

steps.instruction: Stay put. Moving away from your activation point makes you harder to find.
steps.helpDetail: Rescuers are heading to the coordinates broadcast by the beacon. If you must move, leave a highly visible trail or message indicating your direction.
steps.illustrationDescription: Stay put. Moving away from you

steps.instruction: Prepare for a long wait. Weather, terrain, and aircraft availability dictate response time.
steps.helpDetail: An SOS signal guarantees a response is organizing, not that rescue is instantaneous. It may take 4 to 24 hours. Keep surviving.
steps.illustrationDescription: Prepare for a long wait. Weath

techniques.name: Drowning Rescue (Reach-Throw-Row-Go)
techniques.subtitle: Rescue someone in the water without drowning yourself

steps.instruction: REACH: If the victim is close to the edge, extend a stick, paddle, branch, or towel from a secure position.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT offer your hand. A panicked victim has immense strength and will pull you in. Lie flat to lower your center of gravity before reaching.
steps.illustrationDescription: REACH: If the victim is close

steps.instruction: THROW: If they are out of reach, throw anything that floats — a cooler, life ring, jug, or rope.
steps.helpDetail: Throw the object PAST the victim and pull it back toward them. Provide instructions: 'Grab the cooler!' Keep eye contact.
steps.illustrationDescription: THROW: If they are out of reac

steps.instruction: ROW: Use a boat, kayak, or improvised raft to paddle to the victim.
steps.helpDetail: Keep the victim on the leeward (downwind) side. Have them grab the stern/transom of the boat, not the side, to prevent capsizing.
steps.illustrationDescription: ROW: Use a boat, kayak, or imp

steps.instruction: GO (Only if trained): Swimming to a panicked victim is the absolute last resort and highly dangerous.
steps.helpDetail: Only attempt if you are a trained lifesaver. Take a flotation device with you (a towel or buoy) and push it to the victim rather than making physical contact.

steps.instruction: If grabbed: dive straight downward underwater. The victim wants to stay on the surface and will let go.
steps.helpDetail: A drowning person climbs the rescuer like a ladder. Submerging forces them to release their grip so you can reposition.

techniques.name: Sea Dye Marker Deployment
techniques.subtitle: Staining the ocean neon green for search planes

steps.instruction: Keep the dye marker attached to your life vest via the lanyard.
steps.helpDetail: If you drop it in high seas, it is gone forever.
steps.illustrationDescription: Keep the dye marker attached t

steps.instruction: Do NOT deploy the dye until you hear or see an aircraft.
steps.helpDetail: The dye disperses and dilutes in the ocean current over 30 to 45 minutes. Deploying it to an empty sky is a waste.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT deploy the dye until yo

steps.instruction: Tear open the packet and empty the fluorescent uranine powder into the water downwind from you.
steps.helpDetail: The packet immediately turns the water a brilliant, unnatural fluorescent yellow-green.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tear open the packet and empty

steps.instruction: Agitate the water gently to help it spread into a massive slick.
steps.helpDetail: Even a few ounces of powder can create a stain visible from 3,000 feet in the air.
steps.illustrationDescription: Agitate the water gently to he

steps.instruction: Retain the empty packet.
steps.helpDetail: The microscopic residue inside is often enough to create a small second bloom if another plane arrives hours later.

techniques.name: Aerial Parachute Flare
techniques.subtitle: Launching a 30,000 candela meteor to attract ships

steps.instruction: Assess the wind direction.
steps.helpDetail: You must fire the flare slightly downwind so the magnesium slag does not burn holes in your life raft as it falls.

steps.instruction: Wait until a vessel or aircraft is genuinely within visual range.
steps.helpDetail: A parachute flare burns for about 40 seconds. A meteor flare burns for only 5 seconds. Timing is everything.
steps.illustrationDescription: Wait until a vessel or aircraf

steps.instruction: Hold the flare tube firmly in your non-dominant hand, pointed skyward at a 75 to 80-degree angle.
steps.helpDetail: NEVER point it directly straight up (90 degrees) over your own head.
steps.illustrationDescription: Hold the flare tube firmly in

steps.instruction: Pull the firing chain or strike the cap, keeping your face turned entirely away from the tube.
steps.helpDetail: The backblast and sparks can cause severe eye injury.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pull the firing chain or strik

steps.instruction: Once it fires, immediately drop the spent tube in the water.
steps.helpDetail: The plastic casing will be searing hot.

techniques.name: Ground-to-Air Symbols
techniques.subtitle: Writing massive letters to communicate with searchers

steps.instruction: Select a large, open clearing visible from the sky, contrasting strongly with the ground.
steps.helpDetail: A white beach, a green meadow, or a flat expanse of snow.

steps.instruction: Construct symbols at least 10 feet wide and 3 feet thick.
steps.helpDetail: Use rocks on snow, dark pine boughs on sand, or bright clothing pinned to the ground. If you step on the snow to make it, dig deep trenches to create shadow contrast.

steps.instruction: V = Require Assistance.
steps.helpDetail: This is the universal sign for distress. Focus on making one massive 'V' rather than a tiny 'SOS'.

steps.instruction: X = Require Medical Assistance.
steps.helpDetail: If someone is severely injured and will die without immediate medevac, use the X.

steps.instruction: Arrow (->) = Proceeding in this direction.
steps.helpDetail: If you must abandon the area, build an arrow pointing exactly where you are walking so SAR teams can track you.

techniques.name: EPIRB Activation
techniques.subtitle: How to deploy an Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon properly offshore.

steps.instruction: Access the signaling device or method immediately upon realizing distress.
steps.helpDetail: Delaying signals reduces your search radius effectively to zero.

steps.instruction: Execute the standard protocol: How to deploy an Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon properly offshore.
steps.helpDetail: Do not panic or rush the deployment, or you may drop or break the flare/radio.

steps.instruction: Conserve battery/fuel until a target is within maximum range.
steps.helpDetail: Don't fire flares at heavy clouds; don't broadcast on VHF if out of line-of-sight without DSC.

steps.instruction: Maintain the signal aggressively once contact is made.
steps.helpDetail: Keep flashing the mirror or lighting smoke until the rescue asset visually acknowledges you.
