techniques.name: Man Overboard Response
techniques.subtitle: Immediate actions when someone falls into the water from a vessel

steps.instruction: SHOUT 'MAN OVERBOARD!' immediately and point continuously at the person in the water.
steps.helpDetail: Designate one person to do nothing but point at the victim. In open ocean, losing visual contact is almost always fatal.

steps.instruction: Throw flotation immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Life rings, seat cushions, coolers — anything that floats. Do not wait to grab the 'proper' device. Throw whatever is nearest.

steps.instruction: Activate the MOB button on GPS or drop a floating GPS marker.
steps.helpDetail: This records the exact coordinates. In current and wind, the person will drift rapidly from this point.
steps.illustrationDescription: Activate the MOB button on GPS

steps.instruction: Maneuver the vessel to approach the victim FROM DOWNWIND.
steps.helpDetail: Approaching upwind risks drifting the vessel over the victim. Engines in neutral when alongside — propellers kill.

steps.instruction: Get them aboard via the lowest point of the vessel.
steps.helpDetail: Use a swim ladder, sling, or pull them over the transom. Hypothermia sets in within minutes in cold water; they may be unable to help themselves.
steps.illustrationDescription: Get them aboard via the lowest

techniques.name: Emergency Cell Phone Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Maximizing phone usefulness in survival situations

steps.instruction: Switch to Airplane Mode to conserve battery, then enable Wi-Fi only if a network is available.
steps.helpDetail: A phone searching for signal drains the battery 10x faster than Airplane Mode.
steps.illustrationDescription: Switch to Airplane Mode to con

steps.instruction: Text instead of calling.
steps.helpDetail: Text messages require a fraction of the signal strength and battery that a voice call does. Texts will queue and send when signal is briefly available.

steps.instruction: Climb to the highest accessible point to attempt a call.
steps.helpDetail: Cell signals travel line-of-sight. Even 50 feet of elevation gain can make the difference between signal and no signal.

steps.instruction: Dial 911/112 even without a SIM card or service plan.
steps.helpDetail: Emergency calls are legally required to connect on ANY available carrier network, even without a plan. Remove your SIM card to force the phone to search all networks.

steps.instruction: Use the phone's flashlight for signaling at night.
steps.helpDetail: Three short flashes, three long flashes, three short flashes (SOS). The camera flash is visible for hundreds of meters.

techniques.name: Z-Drag (3:1 Haul System)
techniques.subtitle: Moving massive weight with ropes

steps.instruction: Build a bombproof anchor.
steps.helpDetail: Tie your main rope to a massive tree or boulder using a tensionless hitch.

steps.instruction: Attach the load.
steps.helpDetail: Attach the other end of the rope to the trapped vehicle or person.

steps.instruction: Attach a progress capture (Prusik 1).
steps.helpDetail: Tie a Prusik loop onto the main line near the anchor, and clip a carabiner to it.

steps.instruction: Attach the tractor (Prusik 2).
steps.helpDetail: Tie a second Prusik loop further down the line toward the load, and clip a carabiner to it.

steps.instruction: Thread the Z.
steps.helpDetail: Run the tail of the rope through Prusik 2, back through Prusik 1, and pull. You now have a 3-to-1 mechanical advantage.

techniques.name: Breaching Interior Walls
techniques.subtitle: Escaping entrapment inside a building

steps.instruction: Identify standard drywall.
steps.helpDetail: Interior non-load-bearing walls are usually 1/2-inch gypsum drywall, which is highly brittle.

steps.instruction: Find the studs.
steps.helpDetail: Tap the wall. Hollow sounds are empty bays; solid sounds are wooden studs (usually 16 inches apart). Do not strike the studs.

steps.instruction: Kick the lowest point.
steps.helpDetail: Lie on your back and use both feet to mule-kick the drywall right above the baseboard, between two studs.

steps.instruction: Tear the gypsum.
steps.helpDetail: Once a hole is made, pull the drywall chunks toward you. Pulling is easier than pushing against the far wall's paper backing.

steps.instruction: Crawl under electrical wires.
steps.helpDetail: Electrical wires are usually strung horizontally at outlet height. Stay low to avoid them as you squeeze through the 14-inch gap between studs.

techniques.name: Active Shooter Geometry
techniques.subtitle: Tactical evasion and cover utilization

steps.instruction: Evacuate immediately (RUN).
steps.helpDetail: Leave your belongings. Keep your hands visible to law enforcement. Do not run in a straight line if under fire.

steps.instruction: Understand Cover vs. Concealment.
steps.helpDetail: Cover stops bullets (concrete, engine blocks). Concealment only hides you (bushes, drywall, car doors). Seek Cover first.

steps.instruction: Slice the pie around corners.
steps.helpDetail: Do not hug the wall closely when rounding a corner. Take a wide arc to peek and expose as little of your body as possible.

steps.instruction: Barricade deeply (HIDE).
steps.helpDetail: If trapped in a room, lock the door, turn off lights, silence phones (even vibration), and stack heavy furniture against the door.

steps.instruction: Ambush with improvised weapons (FIGHT).
steps.helpDetail: If confronted, commit to absolute violence. Move as a coordinated swarming group. Use fire extinguishers to blind and bludgeon the attacker.

techniques.name: Riot & Mob Evasion
techniques.subtitle: Navigating fluid human crush dynamics

steps.instruction: Move perpendicularly.
steps.helpDetail: Do not try to push directly against the flow of the crowd, nor run directly with it. Move diagonally or perpendicularly toward the edges.

steps.instruction: Avoid choke points.
steps.helpDetail: Stay away from narrow alleys, doorways, fences, and bridges where crowd crushes occur.

steps.instruction: Keep your chest protected.
steps.helpDetail: Fold your arms in a box shape in front of your chest to give your lungs room to expand in a crush.

steps.instruction: Stay on your feet.
steps.helpDetail: If you fall, get up immediately. If you cannot, curl into a tight ball, cover your neck, and create an air pocket with your knees and elbows.

techniques.name: One-Person Litter Drag
techniques.subtitle: Moving a casualty alone across terrain

steps.instruction: Wrap patient in blanket/tarp
steps.helpDetail: Secure them so they won't slide out.

steps.instruction: Grab the head end of the material
steps.helpDetail: Walk backward, dragging the patient.

steps.instruction: Maintain head-uphill orientation
steps.helpDetail: Always drag head-first uphill to prevent blood pooling in the head.

steps.instruction: Use smooth terrain
steps.helpDetail: Avoid dragging over rocks and roots. Clear a path if needed.

techniques.name: Fireman's Carry Technique
techniques.subtitle: Carrying an unconscious person over shoulders

steps.instruction: Roll patient face-down
steps.helpDetail: Grab their right wrist with your left hand.

steps.instruction: Pull patient onto your shoulders
steps.helpDetail: Squat, drape them across your shoulders, and stand using your legs.

steps.instruction: Hold their right arm and right leg
steps.helpDetail: This distributes weight across your shoulders.

steps.instruction: Walk with short, steady steps
steps.helpDetail: Don't run. Maintain balance on uneven terrain.

techniques.name: Two-Person Seat Carry
techniques.subtitle: Carrying a conscious casualty using arms as a seat

steps.instruction: Face each other and grip wrists
steps.helpDetail: Each person grips their own wrist, then the other person's wrist, forming a square.

steps.instruction: Have patient sit on the seat
steps.helpDetail: They wrap arms around both carriers' necks.

steps.instruction: Stand together using a count
steps.helpDetail: 1-2-3, stand. Coordinate to prevent dropping the patient.

steps.instruction: Walk in step
steps.helpDetail: Move together to prevent the seat from shifting.

techniques.name: Casualty Packaging for Transport
techniques.subtitle: Securing injured for safe movement

steps.instruction: Immobilize all known injuries first
steps.helpDetail: Splints, bandages, and padding must be applied before movement.

steps.instruction: Insulate from environment
steps.helpDetail: Hypothermia kills evacuating patients. Wrap in every available layer.

steps.instruction: Secure to litter with straps or cordage
steps.helpDetail: Patient must not slide or roll during transport.

steps.instruction: Mark injuries on patient
steps.helpDetail: Use marker or tape to note injury locations for receiving medical personnel.

techniques.name: Helicopter LZ Marking
techniques.subtitle: Preparing a safe landing zone

steps.instruction: Clear a 100x100 foot area
steps.helpDetail: Remove all loose debris that rotor wash can turn into projectiles.

steps.instruction: Mark the center with a visible panel
steps.helpDetail: Orange smoke, contrasting cloth, or signal mirror.

steps.instruction: Indicate wind direction
steps.helpDetail: Smoke, a windsock, or flag shows the pilot wind direction.

steps.instruction: Approach from DOWNHILL side only
steps.helpDetail: Never approach from uphill—rotor clearance is minimal on the uphill side.

techniques.name: Ground Signal Panel Construction
techniques.subtitle: Building visible signals for aerial search

steps.instruction: Use contrasting materials
steps.helpDetail: Orange or bright colored material on green terrain; dark on snow.

steps.instruction: Make signals at least 10 feet long
steps.helpDetail: Larger is always better for aerial visibility.

steps.instruction: Standard ground-to-air signals
steps.helpDetail: V = Need assistance. X = Need medical. → = Traveling this direction. LL = All is well.

steps.instruction: Place on open, elevated terrain
steps.helpDetail: Hill tops and clearings are visible from the widest angle.

techniques.name: Urban Rubble Rescue Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Finding and extracting survivors in collapsed structures

steps.instruction: Listen/call cycle
steps.helpDetail: Call out for 10 seconds, then SILENCE for 30 seconds. Listen for tapping or voices.

steps.instruction: Mark searched areas
steps.helpDetail: Use USAR marking system: X = searched. / = search in progress.

steps.instruction: Shore as you go
steps.helpDetail: Unstable rubble can shift. Brace with lumber or debris as you tunnel.

steps.instruction: Monitor for secondary collapse
steps.helpDetail: Aftershocks or structure settling can cause further collapse. Post a safety lookout.

techniques.name: Throw Bag Advanced Technique
techniques.subtitle: Upstream pendulum and live-bait rescue

steps.instruction: Throw UPSTREAM of the victim
steps.helpDetail: Current carries the rope to them. Aim 2 body-lengths upstream.

steps.instruction: Brace for load
steps.helpDetail: Wrap rope around your waist or a tree. The victim's weight in current is massive.

steps.instruction: Pendulum to shore
steps.helpDetail: As the victim grabs the rope, the current swings them to your bank.

steps.instruction: Coil for re-throw if first throw misses
steps.helpDetail: Stuff the rope back into the bag quickly for a second throw.

techniques.name: Confined Space Entry Protocols
techniques.subtitle: Safe entry into collapsed or enclosed spaces

steps.instruction: Test atmosphere if possible
steps.helpDetail: Oxygen depletion and toxic gases kill faster than any injury. If no meter, use a candle.

steps.instruction: Maintain contact with outside
steps.helpDetail: Tether line, voice contact, or tug signals.

steps.instruction: Have a backup rescuer ready
steps.helpDetail: Never enter alone. The backup can extract you if you're overcome.

steps.instruction: Monitor for structural movement
steps.helpDetail: Creaking, dust falls, and shifting are signs of imminent collapse.

techniques.name: Simple 2:1 Rope Haul
techniques.subtitle: Mechanical advantage for rescue hauling

steps.instruction: Anchor the standing end of the rope
steps.helpDetail: Fixed to a solid anchor: tree, vehicle, or rock.

steps.instruction: Run rope through a pulley at the load
steps.helpDetail: If no pulley, a carabiner on a sling works.

steps.instruction: Haul on the free end
steps.helpDetail: 2:1 system means 50lbs of pull moves 100lbs of load—but you pull 2 feet of rope per 1 foot of movement.

steps.instruction: Add a prusik for ratcheting
steps.helpDetail: This prevents the load from sliding back when you reset your grip.

techniques.name: Semaphore Flag Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Communicate visually over long distances using arm/flag positions

steps.instruction: Each letter is a different arm position. Arms point like clock hands.
steps.helpDetail: A = left arm at 7 o'clock, right arm down. B = left at 6, right down.

steps.instruction: Learn the distress signals first: 'HELP' and 'SOS'.
steps.helpDetail: Practice in front of a mirror. The receiver sees the mirror image of your position.

steps.instruction: Use brightly colored cloth attached to sticks for visibility.
steps.helpDetail: Flags should contrast with the background. Red on snow, white on dark backgrounds.

steps.instruction: Signal clearly with pauses between letters.
steps.helpDetail: Full arm extension. Hold each position for 2-3 seconds. Return arms to center between letters.

techniques.name: Emergency Log Raft
techniques.subtitle: Build a river-crossing or coastal raft from logs

steps.instruction: Select 6-10 straight, dry logs of similar diameter (6-10 inches).
steps.helpDetail: Dry wood floats better. Green wood is marginal. Test a log: if it floats with your weight, it's good.

steps.instruction: Lay logs parallel. Lash crosspieces at each end using square lashings.
steps.helpDetail: Crosspieces go on top AND bottom of the log platform, sandwiching them.

steps.instruction: Add a raised platform or deck from smaller sticks for keeping gear dry.
steps.helpDetail: Build up from the raft base. Your gear stays high; the raft sits low in water.

steps.instruction: Create a steering oar or pole. Test the raft in shallow water before committing.
steps.helpDetail: A loaded raft is slow and hard to steer. Use a river's current — don't try to paddle against it. Have a rope ready for emergencies.

techniques.name: Improvised Snowshoe Construction
techniques.subtitle: Walk on deep snow without sinking — essential winter travel

steps.instruction: Select two green branches, 4-5 feet long, flexible enough to bend into a teardrop shape.
steps.helpDetail: Willow, birch, or maple are ideal. Heat helps bending — warm over fire.

steps.instruction: Bend each branch into a teardrop frame, lash the overlapping ends.
steps.helpDetail: The pointed end goes behind. Front should be wide and curved up slightly.

steps.instruction: Weave a platform of cordage, strips of bark, or wire across the frame.
steps.helpDetail: Leave the pivot point for your foot — your toe needs to drop through for walking.

steps.instruction: Attach to boots with cordage — pivot point under the ball of your foot.
steps.helpDetail: The binding should let your heel lift free while the shoe stays flat. Walk with a wide stance to avoid overlap.

techniques.name: Travois (Drag Sled) Construction
techniques.subtitle: Transport injured people or heavy loads across terrain

steps.instruction: Find two strong poles 8-10 feet long.
steps.helpDetail: Green hardwood — must support the load without breaking.

steps.instruction: Spread narrow ends in front (pulling end), wide apart at back.
steps.helpDetail: The poles form a V-shape. The wide end drags on the ground.

steps.instruction: Lash crosspieces between the poles to create a platform.
steps.helpDetail: Start 3 feet from the wide end and work forward. Leave the drag ends clear.

steps.instruction: Load attaches to the platform. Pull from the narrow end, or harness to a person/animal.
steps.helpDetail: One person can drag 100+ pounds on a travois. On snow, add a cloth or bark sheet under the crosspieces for a smooth glide.

techniques.name: Perimeter Trip-Wire Alarm System
techniques.subtitle: Early warning system for camp security

steps.instruction: Set trip wires at ankle height across all approach paths.
steps.helpDetail: Use cord, fishing line, or paracord. Must be taut enough to trigger but not visible.

steps.instruction: Attach noise makers: cans with pebbles, shells on cord, or bells.
steps.helpDetail: The alarm must be loud enough to wake you. Multiple cans create more noise.

steps.instruction: Set at approach choke points: gaps in brush, trail intersections, stream crossings.
steps.helpDetail: Don't try to ring the entire camp — focus on natural approach paths.

steps.instruction: Check and maintain daily — weather, wind, and animals can trigger false alarms.
steps.helpDetail: Multiple concentric rings give more warning time. Test by walking the perimeter yourself.

techniques.name: Signal Fire Platform
techniques.subtitle: Pre-built fire platform ready for instant ignition when aircraft heard

steps.instruction: Build a platform of dry wood, elevated off damp ground on a log base.
steps.helpDetail: 3 feet high, 4 feet wide. Must light FAST when needed.

steps.instruction: Cover with a waterproof layer (bark, plastic, large leaves) until needed.
steps.helpDetail: Rain ruins your signal fire. Keep it dry and ready.

steps.instruction: When aircraft heard: remove cover, ignite from pre-positioned fire. Add green vegetation for smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Green vegetation produces white smoke visible against dark backgrounds.

steps.instruction: Three fires in a triangle = international distress signal.
steps.helpDetail: Space fires 100 feet apart in a clear area visible from the air. The triangle pattern is recognized worldwide by search aircraft.

techniques.name: Ground-to-Air Emergency Symbols
techniques.subtitle: Standard symbols recognized by search aircraft worldwide

steps.instruction: V = need assistance. X = need medical help. I = need supplies.
steps.helpDetail: Letters should be at least 10 feet tall, 3 feet wide, with high contrast to ground.

steps.instruction: Build from contrasting materials: dark rocks on light sand, bright fabric on dark earth.
steps.helpDetail: Shadows deepen contrast. Build on flat, open ground visible from the air.

steps.instruction: An arrow (→) indicates direction of travel if you've moved from the signal site.
steps.helpDetail: Searchers follow arrows to find you.

steps.instruction: Stamp or dig letters in snow, sand, or soft earth if no materials available.
steps.helpDetail: Trenches cast shadows visible from high altitude. Wider and deeper = more visible.

techniques.name: Crevasse Self-Rescue
techniques.subtitle: Climb out of a crevasse using prusik knots and self-rescue technique

steps.instruction: Stop falling. Catch your breath. Assess injuries.
steps.helpDetail: If roped: your rope should have arrested the fall. If unroped: you need to climb.

steps.instruction: Attach two prusik slings to the rope above you — one for foot, one for chest/harness.
steps.helpDetail: Texas kick method: slide foot prusik up, weight it, slide chest prusik up, repeat.

steps.instruction: Alternate: slide, weight, slide, weight — inching up the rope.
steps.helpDetail: Rest frequently. This is exhausting. Even 10 feet of climbing takes enormous energy.

steps.instruction: Once at the lip: use an ice axe or arm strength to roll over the edge.
steps.helpDetail: The lip overhang is the hardest part. Kick steps, use the axe, and commit to the roll.

techniques.name: Swift Water Self-Rescue
techniques.subtitle: Survive falling into fast-moving water

steps.instruction: Float on your BACK, feet downstream, toes up.
steps.helpDetail: Feet act as bumpers against rocks. NEVER float head-first downstream.

steps.instruction: Use arms to ferry across the current — angled swimming toward the nearest bank.
steps.helpDetail: Don't fight the current directly. Angle toward shore while the river carries you downstream.

steps.instruction: In rapids: take breaths in troughs between waves, protect head with arms in heavy water.
steps.helpDetail: Big waves will submerge you briefly. Time your breaths.

steps.instruction: Grab onto anything solid: overhanging branch, boulder, bridge pylon.
steps.helpDetail: Once you catch something, the current tries to pull you under. Pull yourself up immediately or let go and keep floating toward shore.

techniques.name: Improvised Stretcher Construction
techniques.subtitle: Carry an injured person safely with minimal materials

steps.instruction: Two strong poles (7+ feet) plus a connecting surface: jackets, tarp, blanket, or poncho.
steps.helpDetail: Test poles by standing on them — they must support patient weight.

steps.instruction: Jacket method: zip/button two jackets closed, thread poles through the sleeves.
steps.helpDetail: Jackets become the stretcher bed. This is the fastest improvised stretcher.

steps.instruction: Blanket method: lay one pole 1/3 from edge, fold excess over. Repeat with second pole.
steps.helpDetail: Patient's weight traps the blanket folds. No tying needed.

steps.instruction: Carry with 4 people, patient head-first uphill. Communicate: 'ready, lift, walk.'
steps.helpDetail: Brief rests every 200-300 meters. Alternate carriers. Monitor patient continuously during transport.

techniques.name: Improvised Flotation Devices
techniques.subtitle: Turn ordinary items into life-saving floats

steps.instruction: Pants: tie legs in knots, wet, inflate by scooping air — becomes a float vest.
steps.helpDetail: Swing waistband overhead and push into water to trap air. Hold waist closed.

steps.instruction: Plastic containers: any sealed container displaces water. Two 2-liter bottles support an adult.
steps.helpDetail: Stuff inside a shirt or tie to your body. Empty milk jugs, gas cans, water bottles all work.

steps.instruction: Coolers, fuel cans, spare tires — all float.
steps.helpDetail: A vehicle spare tire can support 2-3 people in water.

steps.instruction: Backpack floats if sealed and mostly contains clothing or gear (not rocks).
steps.helpDetail: Trap air inside, seal the closure. A survival backpack provides significant buoyancy.

techniques.name: Fire Arrow Ground Signal
techniques.subtitle: Indicate direction of travel for search teams

steps.instruction: Build an arrow on the ground pointing in your direction of travel.
steps.helpDetail: Arrow should be at least 15 feet long, made of high-contrast materials.

steps.instruction: Make the arrow visible from the air: rocks on sand, logs on grass, dug trenches in snow.
steps.helpDetail: Maximum contrast between arrow and background.

steps.instruction: At night: three fires in the direction you're heading.
steps.helpDetail: The line of fires indicates your travel direction. Searchers can extrapolate your position.

steps.instruction: Update the arrow and signals each time you move to a new location.
steps.helpDetail: Leave markers at each camp site with arrows pointing to the next one. Create a trail search teams can follow.

techniques.name: Emergency Whistle Signal Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Standardized whistle patterns for distress and location

steps.instruction: THREE blasts = universal distress signal. Repeat every minute.
steps.helpDetail: Three of anything = distress. Three fires, three shots, three whistle blasts.

steps.instruction: A whistle carries 10x farther than a human voice and requires less energy.
steps.helpDetail: You can whistle for hours. You can shout for minutes before going hoarse.

steps.instruction: Pause and listen between signal sets for response.
steps.helpDetail: If rescuers hear you, they'll respond with two blasts (acknowledgment).

steps.instruction: Improvise a whistle: acorn cap, thick grass blade between thumbs, or hollowed stick.
steps.helpDetail: A pealess whistle works when wet, in cold, and in wind. Carry one on a lanyard.

techniques.name: Adult CPR Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Cardiopulmonary resuscitation — current guidelines

steps.instruction: Confirm unresponsive: tap shoulders, shout. Call for help.
steps.helpDetail: If someone else is present: direct them to call emergency services and get an AED.

steps.instruction: Check for breathing: look, listen, feel for 10 seconds maximum.
steps.helpDetail: Gasping is NOT breathing. No breathing or only gasping = start CPR.

steps.instruction: 30 compressions: center of chest, 2 inches deep, hard and fast (100-120/min).
steps.helpDetail: Push hard — you may feel ribs break. This is expected. Broken ribs heal; cardiac arrest kills.

steps.instruction: 2 rescue breaths: tilt head back, lift chin, seal mouth. Watch for chest rise.
steps.helpDetail: If no AED available: continue 30:2 until help arrives or patient recovers. Hands-only CPR (no breaths) is acceptable if you can't do rescue breathing.

techniques.name: Adult Choking — Heimlich Maneuver
techniques.subtitle: Abdominal thrusts for complete airway obstruction

steps.instruction: Confirm choking: ask 'Can you breathe?' If they can't speak, cough, or breathe — act.
steps.helpDetail: Universal choking sign: hands clutching throat.

steps.instruction: Stand behind the patient. Fist above navel, below ribcage.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap other hand over fist. Feet slightly staggered for stability.

steps.instruction: Deliver sharp INWARD and UPWARD thrusts.
steps.helpDetail: Each thrust should be a distinct attempt to pop the obstruction out. Don't squeeze ribs.

steps.instruction: Repeat until object is expelled or patient becomes unconscious.
steps.helpDetail: If unconscious: lower to ground, begin CPR. Check mouth for visible objects before each breath. Call emergency services.

techniques.name: Water Rescue — Reach-Throw-Row-Go
techniques.subtitle: Rescue a drowning person without becoming a victim yourself

steps.instruction: REACH: extend a pole, branch, shirt, or any long object to the victim.
steps.helpDetail: Stay on solid ground. Brace yourself — the victim will pull hard.

steps.instruction: THROW: if too far to reach, throw a rope, life ring, cooler, or floating object.
steps.helpDetail: Aim upstream of the victim — current will carry it to them. Tie a line for retrieval.

steps.instruction: ROW: if you have a boat, approach with stern toward the victim.
steps.helpDetail: Never let the victim grab the side — they can capsize you. Pull them over the stern.

steps.instruction: GO (swim rescue): LAST RESORT. Take a floatation device. Approach from behind.
steps.helpDetail: Panicking drowning victims will push you under. Bring something for them to grab instead of you. Trained rescuers only.

techniques.name: Improvised Rescue Harness (Swiss Seat)
techniques.subtitle: Rope harness for rappelling or lowering — no gear needed

steps.instruction: Middle a 20-foot rope. Place the midpoint at the small of your back.
steps.helpDetail: Equal lengths on both sides.

steps.instruction: Wrap both ends around to the front, cross them, and pull between your legs from front to back.
steps.helpDetail: The cross creates the seat portion.

steps.instruction: Bring the ends up through the waist wraps on each hip.
steps.helpDetail: Thread up and through, not around.

steps.instruction: Tie off at the front with a square knot backed with overhand safeties.
steps.helpDetail: The carabiner or lowering line clips into the front wrap where the original cross was. This Swiss seat has been used in military and rescue operations for decades.

techniques.name: Rope Traverse (Tyrolean)
techniques.subtitle: Cross a gap using a tensioned rope — river crossings, crevasses

steps.instruction: Anchor the rope on both sides of the gap — bombproof anchors (trees, boulders, multiple points).
steps.helpDetail: The anchor must hold the rope tension PLUS the weight of the person crossing.

steps.instruction: Tension the rope using a 3:1 or 4:1 mechanical advantage system (Z-drag).
steps.helpDetail: Wrap rope through carabiners or around trees to multiply pulling force.

steps.instruction: Clip into the rope with a carabiner and harness. Cross hand-over-hand or with pulleys.
steps.helpDetail: Keep weight below the rope, not above. Use a backup safety line.

steps.instruction: Communication between both sides: establish signals before crossing begins.
steps.helpDetail: Go/stop/emergency signals prevent miscommunication. Last person across is the most difficult — they must detension and retrieve the rope.

techniques.name: Basic Cliff/Ledge Rescue
techniques.subtitle: Get a person off a ledge safely without technical gear

steps.instruction: Assess: can the person climb with assistance, or do they need to be lowered?
steps.helpDetail: If they can climb with a belay (safety rope from above): this is simplest.

steps.instruction: Simple belay: secure rope to a bombproof anchor. Lower end to the patient.
steps.helpDetail: The belayer wraps rope around their body for friction. Patient ties in with a bowline.

steps.instruction: Lowering: seat them in an improvised harness (Swiss seat), lower with controlled friction.
steps.helpDetail: Use a Münter hitch on a carabiner for controlled lowering. Let rope slip through under tension.

steps.instruction: Multiple rope lengths: tie ropes together with double fisherman's knot.
steps.helpDetail: Each knot reduces rope strength by 30%. Plan for total weight including the stretcher if used.

techniques.name: Urban Building Escape (Fire/Collapse)
techniques.subtitle: Evacuate a compromised building safely

steps.instruction: Crawl low: smoke and heat rise. Breathable air is at floor level.
steps.helpDetail: In heavy smoke: you can't see. Follow walls to exits. Count doorways to know your position.

steps.instruction: Check doors before opening: feel with back of hand. Hot door = fire behind it.
steps.helpDetail: Open hot doors = backdraft risk. Find alternate route.

steps.instruction: If exit blocked: seal the room. Stuff wet cloth under the door. Signal from window.
steps.helpDetail: Wet towels block smoke infiltration. Hang a bright cloth from the window.

steps.instruction: Escape from upper floors: tie sheets together for an improvised rope. Use square knots.
steps.helpDetail: Each sheet provides ~5 feet of descent. Lower children first, then evacuate.

techniques.name: Ground-to-Air Vector Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Directing aircraft toward your exact position or safe landing zones

steps.instruction: Identify a large, open clearing devoid of overhanging canopy.
steps.helpDetail: A signal laid in deep brush or trees is invisible from 500 feet AGL (Above Ground Level). Contrast is absolute king in visual signaling.

steps.instruction: Construct a large 'V' (Require Assistance) or 'X' (Require Medical).
steps.helpDetail: Standard international ground-to-air geometric codes. The symbol must be AT LEAST 10 feet long and 3 feet wide per stroke to be legible from typical search altitudes.

steps.instruction: Use 100% contrasting materials.
steps.helpDetail: Use dark logs/pine boughs on white snow. Use white rocks/bleached wood on dark soil. Dig trenches in sand to cast deep black shadows (the AFMAN shadow-trenching method).

steps.instruction: Ensure symmetry.
steps.helpDetail: Nature does not build perfect geometric shapes. The more mathematically perfect your 'V' or 'X' is, the faster the human eye (or sensor) in the aircraft will snap to it.

techniques.name: Improvised Flare & Pyrotechnic Setup
techniques.subtitle: Preparing rapid-ignition night signals

steps.instruction: Do not rely on a standard campfire for signaling aircraft.
steps.helpDetail: Standard fires lack the burst intensity to pierce cockpit glow or high-altitude haze. You need rapid, vertical illumination.

steps.instruction: Construct a 'Signal Tree'.
steps.helpDetail: Locate a solitary, pitch-rich tree (like pine or spruce) in an open area. Pack dry tinder and highly flammable resin/pitch into the lower branches.

steps.instruction: Prepare a reliable, instant ignition source at the exact base.
steps.helpDetail: When you hear engine noise, ignite the base instantly. The entire tree acts as a rapid vertical flare, burning furiously for 2-5 minutes.

steps.instruction: Construct 'V' formation fires.
steps.helpDetail: Three fires arranged in a massive triangle (Distress). Keep the bases loaded with dry wood but covered with bark to stay dry. Light only when aircraft are verified to be in visual range.

techniques.name: Signal Mirror in Jungle Canopy
techniques.subtitle: Deploying a signal mirror effectively in a jungle canopy environment.

steps.instruction: Assess the Jungle Canopy visibility limitations.
steps.helpDetail: The Jungle Canopy presents specific challenges for visual signals (e.g., canopy blocking light, snow blinding contrast).

steps.instruction: Prepare the Signal Mirror.
steps.helpDetail: Ensure the device is clean, functional, or properly constructed (if improvised) before an aircraft is in audible range.

steps.instruction: Execute the Signal Mirror deployment technique.
steps.helpDetail: Follow SERE protocol. For mirrors, flash the horizon continuously. For smoke, ensure high contrast (black on snow, white on dark timber).

steps.instruction: Maintain the signal during flyovers.
steps.helpDetail: Aircraft often fly grid patterns. A single flash or puff is not enough. Re-engage the signal aggressively when the engine noise returns.

steps.helpDetail: The Alpine Ridgeline presents specific challenges for visual signals (e.g., canopy blocking light, snow blinding contrast).

steps.instruction: Prepare the Smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Ensure the device is clean, functional, or properly constructed (if improvised) before an aircraft is in audible range.

steps.instruction: Execute the Smoke deployment technique.
steps.helpDetail: Follow SERE protocol. For mirrors, flash the horizon continuously. For smoke, ensure high contrast (black on snow, white on dark timber).

steps.instruction: Maintain the signal during flyovers.
steps.helpDetail: Aircraft often fly grid patterns. A single flash or puff is not enough. Re-engage the signal aggressively when the engine noise returns.

techniques.name: Drownproofing (Survival Treading)
techniques.subtitle: Naval Special Warfare protocol for conserving energy in open water.

steps.instruction: Assume the resting position.
steps.helpDetail: Float face down in the water, completely relaxed. Let your arms and legs dangle. The air in your lungs provides the buoyancy. Do not fight to keep your head up.

steps.instruction: Prepare to breathe.
steps.helpDetail: When you need air, slowly bring your hands to your face and spread your legs into a scissor-kick position.

steps.instruction: Exhale underwater, then press and snap.
steps.helpDetail: Exhale through your nose strongly. Press DOWN with your hands while simultaneously snapping your legs together sideways (scissor kick). This pops your head out of the water.

steps.instruction: Inhale quickly and return to rest.
steps.helpDetail: Gasp a deep breath through your mouth while your face is out, then immediately drop your face back into the water and go completely limp. This cycle sustains a swimmer for hours holding 20+ lbs of gear.

techniques.name: Improvised Pants Flotation
techniques.subtitle: Inflating trousers to act as a life preserver.

steps.instruction: Remove trousers.
steps.helpDetail: Tread water gently, unzip your pants, pull them off, and zip/button them back up.

steps.instruction: Tie off the legs.
steps.helpDetail: Tie the two pant legs together in a tight square knot as close to the ankles as possible.

steps.instruction: Inflate the trousers.
steps.helpDetail: Sling the waist of the pants forcefully over your head from behind to trap a massive bubble of air in the legs. Alternatively, hold the waist underwater and splash air down into it with your hand.

steps.instruction: Gather the waist.
steps.helpDetail: Pinch the waist tight to trap the air. Slip your head between the inflated pant legs. Keep them wet, as dry fabric leaks air much faster.

techniques.name: H.E.L.P. & Huddle Positions
techniques.subtitle: Delaying severe hypothermia in freezing water.

steps.instruction: Understand the cold shock response.
steps.helpDetail: Freezing water causes an uncontrollable gasping reflex. Focus entirely on keeping your airway clear for the first 3 minutes until the hyperventilation subsides.

steps.instruction: Minimize movement.
steps.helpDetail: Swimming in cold water drains body heat 35% faster than holding still. The water caught in your clothing acts like a wetsuit; swimming flushes that slightly-warmed water away.

steps.instruction: Assume the H.E.L.P. position (Solo).
steps.helpDetail: Heat Escape Lessening Position. Cross your arms tightly over your chest. Draw your knees completely up to your chest to protect your groin. Floating in a tight ball protects the massive arteries in your core/armpits.

steps.instruction: Form a Huddle (Group).
steps.helpDetail: If there are other survivors, form a tight circle facing inward. Wrap your arms around each other's shoulders/waists and intertwine your legs. A 3-person huddle extends survival time by 50%.

techniques.sourceName: US Coast Guard Rescue & Survival Systems Manual

techniques.name: Adrift: Cooler & Heliograph
techniques.subtitle: Deploying Heliograph (Mirror) while clinging to cooler in open water.

steps.instruction: Stabilize the Cooler first.
steps.helpDetail: Before initiating any heliograph sequence, ensure your grip/harness on the cooler is secure in choppy seas.

steps.instruction: Time the Heliograph deployment.
steps.helpDetail: Do not activate the heliograph randomly. Wait until a search aircraft or vessel is definitively heading toward your sector or visually locked.

steps.instruction: Execute Heliograph from the wave crest.
steps.helpDetail: Ocean swells hide ground-level signals. Deploy the heliograph precisely when the cooler carries you to the top of a swell, maximizing horizon line-of-sight.

steps.instruction: Protect remaining signaling cache.
steps.helpDetail: If the asset passes without seeing you, secure the remaining pyrotechnics or devices immediately in waterproof bags. Saltwater spray corrodes firing mechanisms rapidly.

techniques.name: Crevasse Rescue: Figure-8 on a Bight
techniques.subtitle: Integrating the Figure-8 on a Bight perfectly within a crevasse rescue system.

steps.instruction: Evaluate the load vector for crevasse rescue.
steps.helpDetail: Determine exactly how many kilonewtons (kN) of force the crevasse rescue will generate on the anchor points.

steps.instruction: Tie the Figure-8 on a Bight.
steps.helpDetail: Dress the figure-8 on a bight meticulously. A poorly dressed figure-8 on a bight in a crevasse rescue system can degrade the rope's breaking strength by 50%.

steps.instruction: Pre-tension the Figure-8 on a Bight.
steps.helpDetail: Before committing a human life or massive weight to the system, heavily pull-test and inspect the knot from all sides.

steps.instruction: Lock and back up.
steps.helpDetail: In any critical crevasse rescue application, tie an overhand safety knot behind the figure-8 on a bight and ensure the carabiner gates are locked down tight.

techniques.name: Signal Fire in Dense
techniques.subtitle: Deploying signal fire (smoke) from within a Dense to maximize visibility.

steps.instruction: Analyze Dense interference.
steps.helpDetail: In a dense canopy, your signal is fighting terrain occlusion. You must position the signal fire to break the natural visual baseline.

steps.instruction: Prepare the signal fire.
steps.helpDetail: Gather the fuel, shape the panels, or align the device meticulously. You may only have 30 seconds of aircraft visibility over the Dense.

steps.instruction: Trigger the signal fire (smoke).
steps.helpDetail: Wait until the search asset is audibly or visually confirmed heading into your sector before committing the signal.

steps.instruction: Assume the signal was missed.
steps.helpDetail: Never assume a single aircraft flyover saw you. Immediately prepare the next {sname.lower()} or re-arm the current one.

articles.title: How Search & Rescue Works
articles.body:
Understanding SAR operations helps you cooperate with rescuers.

Timeline:
- Someone reports you missing → SAR activation (2-24 hours)
- Initial search: last known position, planned route, nearest trails (Day 1)
- Expanded search: wider area based on travel estimates (Days 2-3)
- Extended search: aircraft, dogs, thermal imaging (Days 3-7)
- After 7 days: search intensity decreases significantly

What helps rescuers:
- Filing a trip plan with someone (exact dates, route, vehicle location)
- Carrying a whistle and mirror
- Staying on planned route
- Staying PUT when lost
- Bright clothing (avoid earth tones in wilderness — wear orange, red, yellow)

Personal Locator Beacons (PLBs) and satellite communicators (like Garmin inReach) send your GPS position to rescue coordination centers worldwide. They work anywhere on Earth with clear sky view. They are the single most effective survival tool you can carry.

articles.title: Visual Signaling Techniques
articles.body:
Signal detection depends on contrast and movement.

Contrast:
- Dark signals on light backgrounds (rocks on snow/sand)
- Light signals on dark backgrounds (clothing on dark rock/forest)
- Flame at night, smoke by day
- Reflective flash in sunlight

Movement:
- Moving flags catch attention better than static signals
- Waving both arms overhead is the international distress gesture
- Standing waving is visible further than ground signals at low angles

Signal priorities (most to least effective):
1. PLB/satellite communicator (100% detection)
2. Signal mirror (50+ mile range in sunlight)
3. Signal fire/smoke (10-50 mile range)
4. Ground-to-air symbols (1-5 mile range)
5. Whistle (1/2 mile range in calm conditions)
6. Voice (100-200 yards)

Always use multiple methods simultaneously. An aircraft might miss your ground signal but see your mirror flash.

articles.title: Using Your Phone for Rescue
articles.body:
Your smartphone is a powerful survival tool if used wisely.

Battery conservation:
- Airplane mode extends battery 10-50×
- Reduce screen brightness to minimum
- Close all apps
- Turn off Bluetooth, WiFi, location services when not needed
- Cold batteries drain faster — keep phone warm inside clothing

Emergency calls:
- 911/112 route through ANY carrier (not just yours)
- Work without a SIM card
- Emergency SOS via satellite (iPhone 14+, newer Android devices)
- Google Pixel: Emergency SOS calls and crash detection

Offline capability:
- GPS works WITHOUT service (airplane mode + GPS = works)
- Download offline maps before trips (Google Maps, AllTrails)
- Compass app works offline
- Camera for documenting route, injuries, wildlife

Last resort: If battery is critical, write a text message with your coordinates, name, situation. Hit send. Even if it doesn't send now, it will queue and send when signal is eventually found.

articles.title: Personal Locator Beacons: The Ultimate Safety Net
articles.body:
A PLB is the single most effective rescue tool ever invented for wilderness use.

How PLBs work:
- Transmit on 406 MHz to COSPAS-SARSAT satellites orbiting Earth
- Signal includes unique ID registered to you (name, emergency contacts, medical info)
- Satellites relay your position to the nearest Rescue Coordination Center
- Position accuracy: 100 meters with GPS-equipped PLBs
- Battery life: 24-48 hours continuous transmission

PLB vs. Satellite Messenger vs. EPIRB:
- PLB: one-way SOS, no subscription, 5-year battery, for land use
- Satellite messenger (inReach, SPOT): two-way messaging, subscription required, rechargeable
- EPIRB: maritime version, auto-activates in water, required on commercial vessels

Registration is critical:
- Register at beacons.noaa.gov (US) or your country's equivalent
- Include medical conditions, emergency contacts, typical activities
- Update annually and whenever your information changes
- Unregistered PLBs still trigger rescue but delay response

False activation: If you accidentally activate, do NOT turn it off. Call 1-800-406-8840 (US) to cancel. Turning it off and on creates multiple search targets.

articles.title: Emergency Radio Communication
articles.body:
Radio communication can reach rescuers far beyond visual or auditory signaling range.

Key frequencies:
- VHF Channel 16 (156.800 MHz): International maritime distress
- 121.5 MHz: Aviation emergency (monitored by all aircraft)
- FRS Channel 1 (462.5625 MHz): Common family radio default
- CB Channel 9 (27.065 MHz): Emergency channel for CB radios
- HAM 146.520 MHz: National simplex calling frequency

MAYDAY format (memorize):
1. MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY
2. This is [name/callsign] (repeat 3 times)
3. MAYDAY [name/callsign]
4. My position is [coordinates or description]
5. Nature of distress: [injury, lost, stranded]
6. Number of persons: [count]
7. Assistance required: [evacuation, medical, supplies]
8. Any other useful information
9. OVER

Radio discipline:
- Keep transmissions short (under 30 seconds)
- Say 'OVER' when done transmitting
- Say 'OUT' when conversation is finished
- Listen before transmitting to avoid blocking other calls

articles.title: Water Rescue Principles
articles.body:
Water rescue follows a strict priority hierarchy: Reach, Throw, Row, Go.

Reach: Extend a branch, pole, paddle, or clothing from shore. Safest option.

Throw: Toss a rope, throw bag, life ring, or any floating object. Second safest.

Row: Use a boat, kayak, surfboard, or improvised raft to reach the person. Moderate risk.

Go: Swim to the person. LAST RESORT. More rescuers drown than victims in untrained swim rescues.

If you must enter water:
- Take a flotation device with you
- Approach from behind (panicking people will push you under)
- Tow by collar or hair, keeping distance from flailing arms
- If grabbed, go underwater — drowning people release and swim up toward air

Cold water considerations:
- 50°F water: 1-3 hours survival time
- 40°F water: 30-90 minutes
- 32°F water: 15-45 minutes
- Cold shock response peaks in first 3 minutes — focus on breathing control
- Swim failure occurs at 3-30 minutes — muscles stop responding

articles.title: Being Found at Night
articles.body:
Night creates unique challenges and opportunities for rescue.

Advantages of night:
- Light sources visible for extreme distances (20+ miles)
- Fire visible from aircraft at 50+ miles altitude
- Flashlight visible for 5-10 miles
- Phone screen visible for 1-2 miles
- Less visual clutter — any light stands out

Disadvantages of night:
- Ground signals invisible
- Color-based signals useless
- Rescue teams may suspend ground searches
- Navigation for self-rescue is dangerous

Night signaling strategy:
1. Maintain the brightest fire possible on high ground
2. Flash SOS continuously with any available light
3. Use reflective materials to catch searchlights
4. Sound signals carry further at night (cooler air is denser)
5. Keep whistle and mirror within arm's reach at all times

If you see search lights or hear helicopters:
- Wave a flashlight or burning stick rapidly
- Blow whistle in bursts of three
- Do NOT run toward the search team — you may injure yourself in darkness. Let them come to you.

articles.title: The Science of Survival Signaling
articles.body:
Effective signaling is the difference between days and weeks of waiting for rescue.

Visibility ranges by method:
- Signal mirror: 50+ miles (brightest non-electronic signal)
- Smoke column: 15-25 miles (calm conditions)
- Signal fire at night: 20+ miles from aircraft
- Ground signal panel: 3-5 miles from aircraft
- Flashlight: 5-10 miles at night
- Whistle: 1-2 miles
- Shouting: 1/4 mile at best

The signal mirror is the single most effective signaling tool. On a clear day, a mirror flash can be seen by aircraft over 50 miles away. Practice aiming before you need it.

Signaling principles:
1. Contrast: your signal must look different from the environment
2. Movement: moving signals catch attention better than static ones
3. Pattern: three of anything = distress (3 fires, 3 blasts, 3 flashes)
4. Duration: signal continuously — aircraft pass overhead in seconds
5. Location: high ground, open areas, away from shadows

Common mistake: people signal for 5 minutes then stop. Aircraft may be 30 minutes away. Signal continuously whenever you hear or see potential rescue.

articles.title: Water Rescue: Core Principles
articles.body:
Water is the most dangerous rescue environment. More rescuers die in water rescues than any other type.

The rescue priority order (RETHROG):
1. REACH: extend a stick, paddle, branch from shore
2. THROW: throw a rope, flotation device, anything buoyant
3. ROW: use a boat, raft, or floating platform
4. GO: swim to the victim (LAST RESORT ONLY)

Why 'GO' is last:
- Drowning victims grab anything — including you
- Cold water saps your strength faster than expected
- Current is stronger than you think
- You become a second victim, doubling the rescue problem

If you must enter water:
- Bring a flotation device between you and the victim
- Approach from behind to avoid being grabbed
- If grabbed: dive deep — victims release and try to surface
- Use a cross-chest carry to tow to shore

Swiftwater rules:
- Never stand in water above your knees in current
- Foot entrapment kills — shuffle, don't step
- If swept away: feet downstream, ride it out to calm water

articles.title: Self-Rescue Navigation Strategy
articles.body:
If you decide to self-evacuate, navigation strategy is critical.

Follow water downstream:
- Streams lead to rivers. Rivers lead to settlements.
- Water provides hydration along the route.
- Terrain is generally easier along waterways.
- Exception: canyon country — rivers can lead into impassable gorges.

Follow power lines or roads:
- Even abandoned roads lead somewhere.
- Walk on utility right-of-ways — they're cleared paths.
- Power lines lead to substations, which are near towns.

Travel strategy:
- Walk during cool hours (dawn, dusk) in hot climates
- Walk during warm hours (midday) in cold climates
- Rest when visibility is poor (fog, dark)
- Travel 50 minutes, rest 10 minutes
- Mark your trail so you don't circle

Pacing:
- Over flat terrain: expect 2-3 miles per hour
- Over rough terrain: expect 1 mile per hour
- Mountainous: expect 1/2 mile per hour
- Add 1 hour per 1,000 feet elevation gain

Stopping criteria: if you reach a road, trail, river, or fence line, follow it. These all lead to civilization.

articles.title: Group Rescue Coordination
articles.body:
Groups have advantages in rescue situations but require coordination.

Assign roles immediately:
- Signal chief: maintains signal fires and panels
- Medical lead: monitors injured and sick
- Resource lead: manages food, water, firewood
- Lookout: watches and listens for rescue

Group signaling advantages:
- More materials for building signals
- Can maintain 24-hour signal watch
- Louder audio signals
- More hands for ground-to-air panels
- Can send scouts while maintaining base camp

Group decision-making:
- Decisions by consensus when possible, leader decides when deadlocked
- NEVER split the group unless sending scouts on a clear mission
- Scouts travel in pairs, carry signal devices, and have a return time
- Missing scouts trigger search protocol, not more scouts

Group morale:
- Maintain schedule and routine
- Assign meaningful tasks to everyone
- Share information openly (hiding bad news erodes trust)
- Celebrate small wins (fire started, water found, signal built)
- Keep idle time busy (improve shelter, build more signals)

articles.title: Search & Rescue: How Rescuers Think
articles.body:
Understanding how SAR teams operate helps you get found faster.

Search activation timeline:
- Overdue report: 4-24 hours after expected return
- SAR alert: 2-6 hours after report
- Field teams deployed: 4-12 hours after alert
- Air assets: daylight hours only (usually)

Search patterns:
- SAR starts at your last known position (LKP)
- They search the most likely route first
- Then expand in concentric circles
- Statistical: 95% of lost persons found within 3 miles of LKP

What helps SAR find you:
1. File a trip plan with route, expected return, and alternative plans
2. Stay on or near your planned route
3. Stay put once lost (moving makes search area exponentially larger)
4. Make yourself visible: clear area, signals, contrast
5. Signal continuously during likely search hours

What hinders SAR:
- No trip plan = no one reports you missing
- Deviating from planned route = searching wrong area
- Moving after being lost = expanding search area
- Camouflaged shelter = invisible from above

The single most important thing: tell someone where you're going and when you'll be back.
